rows.text: All that we are is the result of what we have thought: it is founded on our thoughts, it is made up of our thoughts. If a man speaks or acts with an evil thought, pain follows him, as the wheel follows the foot of the ox that draws the carriage.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person's identity and experiences stem from their thoughts; harmful intentions in speech or action lead directly to suffering, following as inevitably as a cartwheel follows the ox pulling it.
rows.riskNotes: The framing that all suffering stems from one's own thoughts can compound shame for someone already blaming themselves for their pain, implying their hardship is self-created.

rows.text: All that we are is the result of what we have thought: it is founded on our thoughts, it is made up of our thoughts. If a man speaks or acts with a pure thought, happiness follows him, like a shadow that never leaves him.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person's entire condition stems from their thoughts; when speech and action arise from pure intention, lasting well-being naturally follows.
rows.riskNotes: The framing that all outcomes stem from one's own thoughts can land as blaming for someone already tormented by intrusive thoughts or convinced their suffering is their fault.

rows.text: "He abused me, he beat me, he defeated me, he robbed me,"—in those who harbour such thoughts hatred will never cease.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Clinging to memories of being wronged—recalling who harmed you and how—keeps hatred alive rather than letting it end.
rows.riskNotes: For someone processing recent abuse or betrayal, the instruction to release thoughts of wrongdoing can feel like invalidation or pressure to forgive prematurely, suppressing justified anger needed for protection.

rows.text: "He abused me, he beat me, he defeated me, he robbed me,"—in those who do not harbour such thoughts hatred will cease.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Letting go of narratives about being wronged by others causes hatred to end.
rows.riskNotes: For someone processing recent abuse or betrayal, the framing can sound like invalidation or pressure to forgive prematurely, which may suppress justified anger needed for protection and healing.

rows.text: For hatred does not cease by hatred at any time: hatred ceases by love, this is an old rule.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Responding to hatred with more hatred only perpetuates it; the only way to end hatred is through love.
rows.riskNotes: The instruction to respond to harm with love can feel like invalidating pressure toward someone actively processing anger at an abuser or recent relational violation, though the verse itself is framed as a universal truth rather than personal command.

rows.text: The world does not know that we must all come to an end here;—but those who know it, their quarrels cease at once.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Most people ignore the fact that everyone dies, while those who truly grasp this reality stop fighting with others immediately.
rows.riskNotes: The blunt reminder that everyone dies could land heavily on someone recently bereaved, reopening raw awareness of mortality and loss.

rows.text: He who lives looking for pleasures only, his senses uncontrolled, immoderate in his food, idle, and weak, Mara (the tempter) will certainly overthrow him, as the wind throws down a weak tree.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who pursues sensory pleasure without discipline, overeats, and lacks effort will be easily defeated by destructive forces, just as a frail tree collapses in wind.
rows.riskNotes: The passage frames vulnerability as moral failure and predicts certain downfall, which can deepen shame in someone already struggling with self-worth or feeling defeated by circumstances they cannot control.

rows.text: He who lives without looking for pleasures, his senses well controlled, moderate in his food, faithful and strong, him Mara will certainly not overthrow, any more than the wind throws down a rocky mountain.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who avoids seeking sensory pleasures, keeps their senses disciplined, eats moderately, and maintains faith and inner strength cannot be defeated by temptation or destructive forces, just as wind cannot topple solid rock.

rows.text: He who wishes to put on the yellow dress without having cleansed himself from sin, who disregards temperance and truth, is unworthy of the yellow dress.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who adopts religious outward symbols without first purifying their own wrongdoing, and who ignores self-control and honesty, does not deserve to wear those symbols.
rows.riskNotes: The passage frames worthiness as conditional on moral purity, which can intensify shame in someone already feeling defective or unworthy due to past mistakes.

rows.text: But he who has cleansed himself from sin, is well grounded in all virtues, and regards also temperance and truth, he is indeed worthy of the yellow dress.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has eliminated wrongdoing, established themselves in good qualities, and values moderation and honesty genuinely deserves religious ordination.
rows.riskNotes: The framing of moral worthiness as prerequisite for religious belonging can intensify shame in someone already feeling fundamentally flawed or unworthy.

rows.text: They who imagine truth in untruth, and see untruth in truth, never arrive at truth, but follow vain desires.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who mistake falsehood for reality and dismiss what is real will never grasp the truth, instead chasing empty wants.

rows.text: They who know truth in truth, and untruth in untruth, arrive at truth, and follow true desires.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who can accurately distinguish reality from falsehood, and genuine aims from misleading ones, ultimately align themselves with what is real and pursue authentic goals.

rows.text: As rain breaks through an ill-thatched house, passion will break through an unreflecting mind.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A mind that lacks careful attention and self-awareness is easily penetrated by destructive desires, just as a poorly roofed house lets rain pour through.

rows.text: As rain does not break through a well-thatched house, passion will not break through a well-reflecting mind.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A mind that is carefully guarded and examined, like a roof well-maintained with thatch, resists being penetrated by destructive desire.

rows.text: The evil-doer mourns in this world, and he mourns in the next; he mourns in both. He mourns and suffers when he sees the evil of his own work.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who acts badly experiences ongoing distress across their present life and future existence, suffering particularly when they recognize the harm caused by their own actions.
rows.riskNotes: The passage frames suffering as direct consequence of one's own evil actions, which can intensify shame and self-recrimination in someone already blaming themselves for their pain.

rows.text: The virtuous man delights in this world, and he delights in the next; he delights in both. He delights and rejoices, when he sees the purity of his own work.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who lives morally experiences satisfaction both now and after death, feeling genuine pleasure when reflecting on their own good actions.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on seeing purity in one's own work can deepen distress for someone already tormented by feelings of inadequacy or regret about their actions, making their shame feel more pointed and isolating.

rows.text: The evil-doer suffers in this world, and he suffers in the next; he suffers in both. He suffers when he thinks of the evil he has done; he suffers more when going on the evil path.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who acts badly experiences pain in the present life and after death, and feels distress both when remembering their wrong actions and when continuing to do wrong.
rows.riskNotes: The passage's relentless emphasis on suffering as inevitable consequence of wrongdoing can deepen shame and self-punishment in someone already blaming themselves, and its closed loop of suffering-with-no-exit may intensate despair.

rows.text: The virtuous man is happy in this world, and he is happy in the next; he is happy in both. He is happy when he thinks of the good he has done; he is still more happy when going on the good path.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who lives ethically experiences satisfaction both now and later, finding pleasure in remembering past good actions and greater pleasure in continuing to act well.

rows.text: The thoughtless man, even if he can recite a large portion (of the law), but is not a doer of it, has no share in the priesthood, but is like a cowherd counting the cows of others.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who memorizes teachings but doesn't practice them gains no real benefit from that knowledge, comparable to a hired hand merely tallying livestock that belongs to someone else.
rows.riskNotes: The passage harshly compares a non-practitioner to a low-status laborer minding others' property, which can intensify shame in someone already feeling inadequate about their efforts or spiritual worth.

rows.text: The follower of the law, even if he can recite only a small portion (of the law), but, having forsaken passion and hatred and foolishness, possesses true knowledge and serenity of mind, he, caring for nothing in this world or that to come, has indeed a share in the priesthood.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who knows little scripture but has eliminated destructive mental states and achieved inner clarity lives with genuine spiritual attainment, free from concern for worldly or otherworldly rewards.

rows.text: Earnestness is the path of immortality (Nirvana), thoughtlessness the path of death. Those who are in earnest do not die, those who are thoughtless are as if dead already.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Vigilant, purposeful attention leads toward liberation, while careless inattention leads toward spiritual death; those who live with awareness are truly alive, while the distracted exist in a living death.

rows.text: Those who are advanced in earnestness, having understood this clearly, delight in earnestness, and rejoice in the knowledge of the Ariyas (the elect).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who have made real progress in diligent practice take pleasure in that effort itself and find joy in understanding the teachings of the enlightened.

rows.text: These wise people, meditative, steady, always possessed of strong powers, attain to Nirvana, the highest happiness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who are wise, meditative, steady, and strong in their practice reach Nirvana, which is the highest form of happiness.

rows.text: If an earnest person has roused himself, if he is not forgetful, if his deeds are pure, if he acts with consideration, if he restrains himself, and lives according to law,—then his glory will increase.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who makes consistent effort, stays aware, keeps their actions clean, thinks before acting, controls themselves, and follows ethical standards will see their good reputation grow.

rows.text: By rousing himself, by earnestness, by restraint and control, the wise man may make for himself an island which no flood can overwhelm.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who cultivates alertness, disciplined effort, and self-control builds an inner refuge that remains secure against life's overwhelming difficulties.

rows.text: Fools follow after vanity, men of evil wisdom. The wise man keeps earnestness as his best jewel.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Unwise people pursue empty and harmful goals, while the wise person values diligence above all else.

rows.text: Follow not after vanity, nor after the enjoyment of love and lust! He who is earnest and meditative, obtains ample joy.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Pursuing empty pleasures and sensual indulgence leads nowhere; sustained focus and contemplation yield deep satisfaction.

rows.text: When the learned man drives away vanity by earnestness, he, the wise, climbing the terraced heights of wisdom, looks down upon the fools, serene he looks upon the toiling crowd, as one that stands on a mountain looks down upon them that stand upon the plain.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has studied diligently and overcome arrogance through sustained effort gains perspective that allows them to observe others' struggles with calm detachment rather than contempt, analogous to the physical vantage of height over flat ground.
rows.riskNotes: The hierarchical imagery of the wise looking down upon fools and the toiling crowd can reinforce feelings of inadequacy and worthlessness in someone already struggling with self-judgment.

rows.text: Earnest among the thoughtless, awake among the sleepers, the wise man advances like a racer, leaving behind the hack.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who stays alert and diligent surpasses those who are careless or lazy, moving ahead of them like a swift horse outpaces a worn-out one.

rows.text: By earnestness did Maghavan (Indra) rise to the lordship of the gods. People praise earnestness; thoughtlessness is always blamed.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Diligence leads to high achievement, as shown by a god who attained supremacy through earnestness. Carelessness is universally criticized.

rows.text: A Bhikshu (mendicant) who delights in earnestness, who looks with fear on thoughtlessness, moves about like fire, burning all his fetters, small or large.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A committed practitioner who values diligence and regards carelessness as dangerous will progressively overcome all obstacles to liberation, regardless of their size.

rows.text: A Bhikshu (mendicant) who delights in reflection, who looks with fear on thoughtlessness, cannot fall away (from his perfect state)—he is close upon Nirvana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A committed practitioner who values careful reflection and regards carelessness with genuine concern maintains spiritual progress and nears liberation.

rows.text: As a fletcher makes straight his arrow, a wise man makes straight his trembling and unsteady thought, which is difficult to guard, difficult to hold back.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A skilled person disciplines their restless, hard-to-control mind with the same deliberate care an arrow-maker uses to straighten an arrow.

rows.text: As a fish taken from his watery home and thrown on dry ground, our thought trembles all over in order to escape the dominion of Mara (the tempter).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The mind, when pulled from its habitual environment of desire and distraction, struggles desperately to return to familiar patterns of craving and delusion, just as a fish out of water thrashes to get back.
rows.riskNotes: The image of a fish gasping and thrashing out of water can intensify bodily panic sensations in someone already experiencing panic, making the metaphor feel viscerally distressing rather than illustrative.

rows.text: It is good to tame the mind, which is difficult to hold in and flighty, rushing wherever it listeth; a tamed mind brings happiness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Controlling the mind is valuable because the mind naturally wanders and resists restraint; bringing it under control produces well-being.

rows.text: Let the wise man guard his thoughts, for they are difficult to perceive, very artful, and they rush wherever they list: thoughts well guarded bring happiness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person of discernment should carefully monitor their own thoughts because thoughts are subtle, clever, and wander freely; keeping thoughts under control leads to well-being.

rows.text: Those who bridle their mind which travels far, moves about alone, is without a body, and hides in the chamber (of the heart), will be free from the bonds of Mara (the tempter).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Controlling the mind, which operates independently and invisibly, leads to liberation from temptation and suffering.

rows.text: If a man's thoughts are unsteady, if he does not know the true law, if his peace of mind is troubled, his knowledge will never be perfect.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Mental instability and ignorance of fundamental principles prevent a person from achieving complete understanding or clarity.

rows.text: If a man's thoughts are not dissipated, if his mind is not perplexed, if he has ceased to think of good or evil, then there is no fear for him while he is watchful.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person whose thinking is focused rather than scattered, whose mind is clear rather than confused, and who has moved beyond judging experiences as good or bad, will not experience fear as long as they remain alert and aware.

rows.text: Knowing that this body is (fragile) like a jar, and making this thought firm like a fortress, one should attack Mara (the tempter) with the weapon of knowledge, one should watch him when conquered, and should never rest.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should recognize the body's fragility, fortify their mind against temptation, persistently apply understanding to overcome inner obstacles, and remain vigilant even after apparent success rather than relaxing discipline.

rows.text: Before long, alas! this body will lie on the earth, despised, without understanding, like a useless log.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The human body quickly becomes a discarded, unvalued corpse once consciousness departs, underscoring the urgency of awareness before death.
rows.riskNotes: Graphic image of a corpse-like body as worthless and despised can intensify death anxiety and self-disgust in those already fearing mortality or feeling their existence is meaningless.

rows.text: Whatever a hater may do to a hater, or an enemy to an enemy, a wrongly-directed mind will do us greater mischief.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: External hostility from others causes less harm than the damage inflicted by one's own misdirected or malicious thoughts.

rows.text: Not a mother, not a father will do so much, nor any other relative; a well-directed mind will do us greater service.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: No family member, however devoted, can benefit a person as much as a mind that is properly trained and guided.

rows.text: Who shall overcome this earth, and the world of Yama (the lord of the departed), and the world of the gods? Who shall find out the plainly shown path of virtue, as a clever man finds out the (right) flower?
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The passage asks who can transcend the realms of the living, the dead, and the divine, and who can discern the clear path of virtue with the same skill a knowledgeable person uses to select the correct flower.

rows.text: The disciple will overcome the earth, and the world of Yama, and the world of the gods. The disciple will find out the plainly shown path of virtue, as a clever man finds out the (right) flower.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A dedicated practitioner can transcend all realms of existence and discern the clear path of ethical living, much as a skilled person identifies the correct flower among many.

rows.text: He who knows that this body is like froth, and has learnt that it is as unsubstantial as a mirage, will break the flower-pointed arrow of Mara, and never see the king of death.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who recognizes the body as insubstantial and illusory overcomes the seductions that lead to repeated death.
rows.riskNotes: The imagery of bodily dissolution and death could retraumatize someone recently bereaved or processing raw loss, though the passage ultimately points toward liberation rather than dwelling in morbidity.

rows.text: Death carries off a man who is gathering flowers and whose mind is distracted, as a flood carries off a sleeping village.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person absorbed in trivial pleasures and mentally unfocused is swept away by death as suddenly and completely as a flood overwhelms an unprepared sleeping village.
rows.riskNotes: The sudden, violent image of death sweeping away the unaware person could retraumatize someone recently bereaved or amplify fatalistic dread in someone already contemplating ending their life.

rows.text: Death subdues a man who is gathering flowers, and whose mind is distracted, before he is satiated in his pleasures.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person whose attention is scattered while pursuing enjoyments gets overtaken by death before ever feeling fulfilled.
rows.riskNotes: The sudden image of death cutting short pleasure could retraumatize someone recently bereaved or amplify hopelessness in those already contemplating ending their life.

rows.text: As the bee collects nectar and departs without injuring the flower, or its colour or scent, so let a sage dwell in his village.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A wise person should live among others so gently that they take what they need without causing any harm or leaving any trace of their presence, just as a bee gathers honey without damaging the flower.

rows.text: Not the perversities of others, not their sins of commission or omission, but his own misdeeds and negligences should a sage take notice of.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A wise person focuses on their own wrong actions and failures rather than monitoring and judging the faults of other people.
rows.riskNotes: The instruction to focus only on one's own faults can amplify shame in those already self-critical, and may pressure abuse survivors to ignore others' wrongdoing against them.

rows.text: Like a beautiful flower, full of colour, but without scent, are the fine but fruitless words of him who does not act accordingly.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Words without matching actions are like flowers that look attractive but have no fragrance—they fail to achieve their purpose.

rows.text: But, like a beautiful flower, full of colour and full of scent, are the fine and fruitful words of him who acts accordingly.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Words become genuinely beautiful and productive only when backed by corresponding action; eloquence without practice is hollow.

rows.text: As many kinds of wreaths can be made from a heap of flowers, so many good things may be achieved by a mortal when once he is born.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has been born has the capacity to accomplish many different good things, much like many different wreaths can be crafted from a single pile of flowers.

rows.text: The scent of flowers does not travel against the wind, nor (that of) sandal-wood, or of Tagara and Mallika flowers; but the odour of good people travels even against the wind; a good man pervades every place.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Physical fragrances are limited by wind direction, but the moral influence of good people spreads everywhere regardless of obstacles.

rows.text: Sandal-wood or Tagara, a lotus-flower, or a Vassiki, among these sorts of perfumes, the perfume of virtue is unsurpassed.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Among all prized fragrances, the quality of virtuous living surpasses every material scent.

rows.text: Mean is the scent that comes from Tagara and sandal-wood;—the perfume of those who possess virtue rises up to the gods as the highest.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Physical fragrances are inferior compared to the spiritual 'scent' of moral character, which reaches the highest realm.

rows.text: Of the people who possess these virtues, who live without thoughtlessness, and who are emancipated through true knowledge, Mara, the tempter, never finds the way.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who cultivate good qualities, avoid careless living, and achieve liberation through genuine understanding remain beyond the reach of temptation and spiritual obstruction.

rows.text: As on a heap of rubbish cast upon the highway the lily will grow full of sweet perfume and delight, thus the disciple of the truly enlightened Buddha shines forth by his knowledge among those who are like rubbish, among the people that walk in darkness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A student of the Buddha can develop remarkable clarity and virtue even when surrounded by people who lack understanding or live without awareness, just as a fragrant lotus grows from a pile of refuse.
rows.riskNotes: The comparison of people to 'rubbish' and those 'who walk in darkness' could deepen shame for someone already feeling worthless or defective, framing others as contemptible rather than suffering.

rows.text: Long is the night to him who is awake; long is a mile to him who is tired; long is life to the foolish who do not know the true law.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: For people lacking understanding, ordinary experiences feel interminable—time drags, distances stretch, and existence itself becomes a burden.
rows.riskNotes: The verse labels certain people as foolish and implies their suffering stems from ignorance, which could deepen shame in someone already feeling defective or self-critical.

rows.text: If a traveller does not meet with one who is his better, or his equal, let him firmly keep to his solitary journey; there is no companionship with a fool.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person journeying through life should remain alone rather than associate with someone inferior; no genuine companionship exists with a foolish person.

rows.text: "These sons belong to me, and this wealth belongs to me," with such thoughts a fool is tormented. He himself does not belong to himself; how much less sons and wealth?
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person suffers when they claim ownership over family and possessions, failing to recognize that they themselves lack permanent, autonomous existence.

rows.text: The fool who knows his foolishness, is wise at least so far. But a fool who thinks himself wise, he is called a fool indeed.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who recognizes their own lack of understanding has a basic form of wisdom. Someone who lacks understanding but believes themselves knowledgeable is truly foolish.
rows.riskNotes: The repeated use of "fool" as identity-label could deepen shame for someone already harshly self-critical, though the verse's actual message is self-awareness as wisdom.

rows.text: If a fool be associated with a wise man even all his life, he will perceive the truth as little as a spoon perceives the taste of soup.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Mere proximity to wisdom does not transform someone who lacks the capacity or willingness to learn; the fool remains unaffected by the wise person's presence just as a spoon cannot taste the food it touches.
rows.riskNotes: The explicit labeling of someone as a 'fool' and the implication of inherent incapacity to learn could reinforce feelings of worthlessness or unchangeable deficiency in someone already struggling with deep shame.

rows.text: If an intelligent man be associated for one minute only with a wise man, he will soon perceive the truth, as the tongue perceives the taste of soup.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Brief contact with a wise person allows someone intelligent to quickly recognize what is true, just as the tongue immediately discerns flavor.

rows.text: Fools of little understanding have themselves for their greatest enemies, for they do evil deeds which must bear bitter fruits.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who lack wisdom harm themselves most of all, because the harmful actions they commit produce painful results for them.
rows.riskNotes: The framing of suffering people as fools who are their own worst enemies can deepen shame in someone already blaming themselves for their difficulties.

rows.text: That deed is not well done of which a man must repent, and the reward of which he receives crying and with a tearful face.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: An action that leads to later regret and painful results is not a good action. The true measure of a deed's quality is the aftermath it produces for the doer.

rows.text: No, that deed is well done of which a man does not repent, and the reward of which he receives gladly and cheerfully.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: An action is genuinely good when the person later feels no regret about it and experiences its results with gladness rather than remorse.

rows.text: As long as the evil deed done does not bear fruit, the fool thinks it is like honey; but when it ripens, then the fool suffers grief.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who acts badly may feel fine while the harmful results remain hidden, but suffers once those consequences finally appear.
rows.riskNotes: The framing of suffering as ripened fruit of one's own evil deeds can land as moralistic causation on someone already blaming themselves for their pain.

rows.text: Let a fool month after month eat his food (like an ascetic) with the tip of a blade of Kusa grass, yet he is not worth the sixteenth particle of those who have well weighed the law.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Extreme physical austerity without understanding the teachings is worthless compared to even a small amount of genuine comprehension. Ritual severity alone does not transform a person if they fail to grasp the actual principles.
rows.riskNotes: The verse explicitly devalues a person's spiritual efforts as worthless compared to others who understand the law, which can deepen shame in someone already feeling inadequate or self-critical about their practice or worth.

rows.text: An evil deed, like newly-drawn milk, does not turn (suddenly); smouldering, like fire covered by ashes, it follows the fool.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Harmful actions may not show immediate effects, but they accumulate and pursue the person who does them, like hidden embers that continue burning under ash.
rows.riskNotes: The metaphor of hidden, smoldering evil that inevitably pursues the fool can intensify shame and self-condemnation in someone already tormented by their own perceived wrongdoing.

rows.text: And when the evil deed, after it has become known, brings sorrow to the fool, then it destroys his bright lot, nay, it cleaves his head.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A foolish person suffers when their wrongdoing becomes public; the resulting shame and ruin damages their reputation and causes them intense distress.
rows.riskNotes: The imagery of a deed cleaving the head and destroying one's lot can intensify shame and self-punishment in someone already blaming themselves, and the focus on sorrow as destruction may deepen distress for those recently bereaved or grieving.

rows.text: Let the fool wish for a false reputation, for precedence among the Bhikshus, for lordship in the convents, for worship among other people!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A fool may actively desire superficial status markers—recognition, rank, authority over others, and public adoration—rather than genuine spiritual development.
rows.riskNotes: The verse's contempt for the fool's desires for recognition and status could wound someone already harshly judging their own ambition or social standing, reinforcing self-criticism rather than offering perspective.

rows.text: "May both the layman and he who has left the world think that this is done by me; may they be subject to me in everything which is to be done or is not to be done," thus is the mind of the fool, and his desire and pride increase.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A foolish person wants everyone, whether layperson or renunciant, to believe they are responsible for all actions and to obey their authority in every matter, which only inflates their desire and pride.

rows.text: "One is the road that leads to wealth, another the road that leads to Nirvana;" if the Bhikshu, the disciple of Buddha, has learnt this, he will not yearn for honour, he will strive after separation from the world.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A Buddhist disciple who understands that worldly success and spiritual liberation follow different paths will abandon the pursuit of status and instead work toward detachment from worldly life.

rows.text: If you see an intelligent man who tells you where true treasures are to be found, who shows what is to be avoided, and administers reproofs, follow that wise man; it will be better, not worse, for those who follow him.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should seek out and follow someone who can identify what is genuinely valuable, point out what to avoid, and offer necessary criticism, because doing so leads to improvement rather than harm.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on needing reproof and guidance from a superior figure can reinforce shame in those already feeling fundamentally flawed, and may echo dynamics of harsh self-criticism or past experiences of being told they are the problem.

rows.text: Let him admonish, let him teach, let him forbid what is improper!—he will be beloved of the good, by the bad he will be hated.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who corrects others—warning, instructing, and prohibiting wrong behavior—will earn the affection of good people and the hostility of bad ones.
rows.riskNotes: The verse normalizes being hated by 'the bad' as a consequence of speaking up, which could reinforce isolation or self-doubt for someone already wounded by relational harm who has been punished for setting boundaries.

rows.text: Do not have evil-doers for friends, do not have low people for friends: have virtuous people for friends, have for friends the best of men.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The passage advises choosing friends based on moral character, recommending association with virtuous and excellent people while avoiding those who do harm or lack integrity.
rows.riskNotes: For someone currently trapped in or recovering from relational harm, the directive to cut ties may echo abuser-isolation tactics or intensify self-blame for not having left sooner, even though the verse intends protective guidance.

rows.text: He who drinks in the law lives happily with a serene mind: the sage rejoices always in the law, as preached by the elect (Ariyas).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who internalizes the teachings lives with calm contentment; the wise find lasting satisfaction in the doctrine taught by enlightened beings.

rows.text: Well-makers lead the water (wherever they like); fletchers bend the arrow; carpenters bend a log of wood; wise people fashion themselves.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as skilled craftspeople shape raw materials into useful forms, intelligent people actively work to develop and improve themselves rather than remaining as they are.

rows.text: As a solid rock is not shaken by the wind, wise people falter not amidst blame and praise.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People with wisdom maintain inner stability and do not lose their balance when others criticize or praise them.

rows.text: Wise people, after they have listened to the laws, become serene, like a deep, smooth, and still lake.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who are wise become calm and undisturbed after hearing teachings, comparable to a lake that is deep, untroubled, and motionless.

rows.text: Good people walk on whatever befall, the good do not prattle, longing for pleasure; whether touched by happiness or sorrow wise people never appear elated or depressed.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People of character maintain steady composure through life's fluctuations. They do not chase pleasure or react dramatically to either good fortune or misfortune.

rows.text: If, whether for his own sake, or for the sake of others, a man wishes neither for a son, nor for wealth, nor for lordship, and if he does not wish for his own success by unfair means, then he is good, wise, and virtuous.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who refrains from desiring children, wealth, power, or personal advancement through unethical methods demonstrates genuine goodness, wisdom, and moral integrity.

rows.text: Few are there among men who arrive at the other shore (become Arhats); the other people here run up and down the shore.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Spiritual attainment is rare; most people remain unliberated, cycling through mundane existence without reaching the goal.

rows.text: But those who, when the law has been well preached to them, follow the law, will pass across the dominion of death, however difficult to overcome.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who actually practice well-taught teachings can overcome mortality, which is otherwise insurmountable.

rows.text: A wise man should leave the dark state (of ordinary life), and follow the bright state (of the Bhikshu). After going from his home to a homeless state, he should in his retirement look for enjoyment where there seemed to be no enjoyment. Leaving all pleasures behind, and calling nothing his own, the wise man should purge himself from all the troubles of the mind.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A wise person transitions from ordinary life to the disciplined, possession-free life of a wandering ascetic, finding fulfillment in what others see as deprivation, and clearing away mental disturbances by relinquishing all claims of ownership and pleasure.

rows.text: Those whose mind is well grounded in the (seven) elements of knowledge, who without clinging to anything, rejoice in freedom from attachment, whose appetites have been conquered, and who are full of light, are free (even) in this world.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who have developed understanding, let go of all grasping, find satisfaction in non-attachment, overcome desires, and become spiritually illuminated achieve liberation during life itself rather than only after death.

rows.text: There is no suffering for him who has finished his journey, and abandoned grief, who has freed himself on all sides, and thrown off all fetters.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has completed their spiritual path, released all sorrow, and broken every bond experiences no further suffering.

rows.text: They depart with their thoughts well-collected, they are not happy in their abode; like swans who have left their lake, they leave their house and home.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who have gathered and focused their minds do not find satisfaction in staying put; they move on from their homes as naturally as swans leaving a lake behind.
rows.riskNotes: The imagery of leaving home and the quiet sadness of 'not happy in their abode' could sharpen the ache of recent loss for someone freshly grieving a home, relationship, or person.

rows.text: Men who have no riches, who live on recognised food, who have perceived void and unconditioned freedom (Nirvana), their path is difficult to understand, like that of birds in the air.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who own nothing, live simply, and have realized nirvana move through the world in ways that others cannot track or comprehend, like birds leaving no trace in the sky.

rows.text: He whose appetites are stilled, who is not absorbed in enjoyment, who has perceived void and unconditioned freedom (Nirvana), his path is difficult to understand, like that of birds in the air.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has extinguished desires, no longer clings to pleasure, and has realized the unconditioned state of liberation lives in a way that others cannot easily comprehend, comparable to birds leaving no trace in the sky.

rows.text: The gods even envy him whose senses, like horses well broken in by the driver, have been subdued, who is free from pride, and free from appetites.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has trained their senses with disciplined control, eliminated pride, and overcome desires becomes so exemplary that even divine beings admire that achievement.

rows.text: Such a one who does his duty is tolerant like the earth, like Indra's bolt; he is like a lake without mud; no new births are in store for him.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who fulfills their spiritual obligations embodies patience comparable to the earth's steadiness, possesses decisive moral strength, and maintains inner clarity without the turbidity that leads to continued rebirth.

rows.text: His thought is quiet, quiet are his word and deed, when he has obtained freedom by true knowledge, when he has thus become a quiet man.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has achieved liberation through genuine understanding experiences stillness in thinking, speaking, and acting.

rows.text: The man who is free from credulity, but knows the uncreated, who has cut all ties, removed all temptations, renounced all desires, he is the greatest of men.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has abandoned blind belief, understood ultimate reality, severed all bonds, eliminated enticements, and given up every wanting is considered supreme among human beings.

rows.text: In a hamlet or in a forest, in the deep water or on the dry land, wherever venerable persons (Arhanta) dwell, that place is delightful.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The presence of spiritually accomplished people makes any location—whether village or wilderness, wet or dry—pleasant to be in.

rows.text: Forests are delightful; where the world finds no delight, there the passionless will find delight, for they look not for pleasures.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People free from desire take satisfaction in solitary natural places that ordinary people find boring or uncomfortable, because they do not seek sensory pleasure.

rows.text: Even though a speech be a thousand (of words), but made up of senseless words, one word of sense is better, which if a man hears, he becomes quiet.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A single meaningful word that brings calm to the listener is more valuable than thousands of empty words.

rows.text: Even though a Gatha (poem) be a thousand (of words), but made up of senseless words, one word of a Gatha is better, which if a man hears, he becomes quiet.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A single meaningful word that brings calm to the listener surpasses a thousand empty words, no matter how elaborate the poem.

rows.text: Though a man recite a hundred Gathas made up of senseless words, one word of the law is better, which if a man hears, he becomes quiet.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Memorizing many meaningless verses is less valuable than hearing a single meaningful teaching that brings inner stillness. The quality and impact of what one hears matters more than quantity or ritual recitation.

rows.text: If one man conquer in battle a thousand times thousand men, and if another conquer himself, he is the greatest of conquerors.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: External military victory over vast armies is less significant than the internal victory of mastering one's own impulses and reactions.

rows.text: One's own self conquered is better than all other people; not even a god, a Gandharva, not Mara with Brahman could change into defeat the victory of a man who has vanquished himself, and always lives under restraint.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Overcoming one's own impulses and living with discipline is a greater achievement than any external victory; such self-conquest cannot be undone by any power.
rows.riskNotes: The framing of self as enemy to be conquered and the valorization of self-restraint can reinforce harsh self-criticism in those already struggling with self-worth, making them feel they are failing if they cannot control themselves.

rows.text: If a man for a hundred years sacrifice month after month with a thousand, and if he but for one moment pay homage to a man whose soul is grounded (in true knowledge), better is that homage than sacrifice for a hundred years.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A brief moment of genuine respect toward someone who truly understands reality is more valuable than a century of elaborate ritual offerings.

rows.text: If a man for a hundred years worship Agni (fire) in the forest, and if he but for one moment pay homage to a man whose soul is grounded (in true knowledge), better is that homage than sacrifice for a hundred years.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A single moment of genuine respect toward someone who truly understands reality outweighs a century of ritual fire worship performed in isolation.

rows.text: Whatever a man sacrifice in this world as an offering or as an oblation for a whole year in order to gain merit, the whole of it is not worth a quarter (a farthing); reverence shown to the righteous is better.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Religious rituals and offerings made over an entire year produce less merit than a single act of respect directed toward a morally upright person.

rows.text: He who always greets and constantly reveres the aged, four things will increase to him, viz. life, beauty, happiness, power.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Showing consistent respect and deference toward older people is said to bring four benefits: longer life, physical attractiveness, happiness, and influence or strength.

rows.text: But he who lives a hundred years, vicious and unrestrained, a life of one day is better if a man is virtuous and reflecting.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A single day lived with moral discipline and self-awareness outweighs a century lived without ethical control or reflection.

rows.text: And he who lives a hundred years, ignorant and unrestrained, a life of one day is better if a man is wise and reflecting.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A single day of wise, self-aware living outweighs a century of ignorant, unrestrained existence. The passage values quality of mental life over mere duration.

rows.text: And he who lives a hundred years, idle and weak, a life of one day is better if a man has attained firm strength.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A single day of vigorous, purposeful living surpasses a century of passive, feeble existence. The quality of exertion matters more than the quantity of time.

rows.text: And he who lives a hundred years, not seeing beginning and end, a life of one day is better if a man sees beginning and end.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A single day of understanding the full cycle of existence surpasses a century of living in ignorance of how things arise and cease. The passage values insight into the nature of conditioned reality over mere longevity without comprehension.

rows.text: And he who lives a hundred years, not seeing the immortal place, a life of one day is better if a man sees the immortal place.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A single day of perceiving liberation from death outweighs a century of ordinary life without that insight. The verse values direct experiential understanding of what transcends mortality over mere longevity.

rows.text: And he who lives a hundred years, not seeing the highest law, a life of one day is better if a man sees the highest law.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A single day of comprehending ultimate truth surpasses a century lived in ignorance of it.

rows.text: If a man would hasten towards the good, he should keep his thought away from evil; if a man does what is good slothfully, his mind delights in evil.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who wants to move toward good must actively direct their attention away from harmful things; when they approach good deeds lazily, their mind naturally turns toward harmful things instead.
rows.riskNotes: The framing of moral effort as a race one can fail, and the implication that slothful good-doers secretly delight in evil, can intensify shame for someone already struggling with motivation or self-worth.

rows.text: If a man commits a sin, let him not do it again; let him not delight in sin: pain is the outcome of evil.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Doing wrong once is bad enough; repeating it or enjoying it only brings more suffering, since harmful actions naturally produce painful results.
rows.riskNotes: The framing of sin and the imperative not to delight in it can amplify shame for someone already struggling with self-condemnation, making them feel watched and judged for their wrongdoing rather than supported.

rows.text: If a man does what is good, let him do it again; let him delight in it: happiness is the outcome of good.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Repeating good actions and taking satisfaction in them leads to happiness.

rows.text: Even an evil-doer sees happiness as long as his evil deed has not ripened; but when his evil deed has ripened, then does the evil-doer see evil.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Wrongdoing may appear to bring good fortune while its effects remain delayed, but suffering arrives inevitably once the consequences fully develop.
rows.riskNotes: For someone already tormented by guilt or convinced they are fundamentally bad, the framing of evil deeds ripening into inevitable suffering can intensify shame spirals and confirm their worst fears about themselves.

rows.text: Even a good man sees evil days, as long as his good deed has not ripened; but when his good deed has ripened, then does the good man see happy days.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who acts well may still experience hardship until the positive effects of their actions fully develop; once those effects mature, good outcomes follow.
rows.riskNotes: The framing that suffering persists until an unseen future reward could deepen hopelessness for someone already feeling trapped in unending hardship.

rows.text: Let no man think lightly of evil, saying in his heart, It will not come nigh unto me. Even by the falling of water-drops a water-pot is filled; the fool becomes full of evil, even if he gather it little by little.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Small, repeated harmful acts accumulate over time into serious moral damage, just as tiny drops eventually fill a vessel. Someone who assumes they are safe from consequences because their wrongdoing seems minor is mistaken.
rows.riskNotes: The metaphor of gradual accumulation of evil and the label "fool" can intensify shame in someone already harshly self-critical about their mistakes or moral worth.

rows.text: Let no man think lightly of good, saying in his heart, It will not come nigh unto me. Even by the falling of water-drops a water-pot is filled; the wise man becomes full of good, even if he gather it little by little.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
