rows.summary: Small, repeated acts of goodness accumulate over time to fill a person with virtue, just as tiny drops of water eventually fill a pot. One should not dismiss minor good deeds as insignificant or assume they have no effect.

rows.text: Let a man avoid evil deeds, as a merchant, if he has few companions and carries much wealth, avoids a dangerous road; as a man who loves life avoids poison.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should steer clear of harmful actions with the same cautious care that a vulnerable merchant avoids a risky route, or that someone who values survival avoids poison.

rows.text: He who has no wound on his hand, may touch poison with his hand; poison does not affect one who has no wound; nor is there evil for one who does not commit evil.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person without injury can handle poison safely; likewise, harm does not befall those who do not engage in harmful actions. The verse draws a parallel between physical immunity and moral safety through abstaining from wrongdoing.
rows.riskNotes: The wound metaphor implies that suffering only comes to those with pre-existing moral flaws, which can reinforce shame in someone already blaming themselves for their hardships.

rows.text: If a man offend a harmless, pure, and innocent person, the evil falls back upon that fool, like light dust thrown up against the wind.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Harming someone who has done no wrong brings harm back onto the aggressor, as naturally as dust thrown into the wind returns to the thrower.
rows.riskNotes: The framing that harm to the innocent 'falls back' on the offender can feel invalidating to someone who has experienced real harm and seen no justice, implying cosmic balance that their lived experience contradicts.

rows.text: Some people are born again; evil-doers go to hell; righteous people go to heaven; those who are free from all worldly desires attain Nirvana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The verse presents four outcomes determined by a person's actions and mental state: ordinary rebirth for most, hell for those who do evil, heaven for the righteous, and final liberation for those who have eliminated all craving.
rows.riskNotes: The binary afterlife framing could intensulate existential anxiety for someone recently bereaved who is processing where a loved one 'went' or fearing their own moral standing.

rows.text: Not in the sky, not in the midst of the sea, not if we enter into the clefts of the mountains, is there known a spot in the whole world where a man might be freed from an evil deed.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: There is no physical place anywhere on earth or beyond where someone can escape the results of their own harmful actions.
rows.riskNotes: The absolute, inescapable framing of evil deeds could intensify crushing guilt or hopelessness in someone already tormented by their own actions, offering no path forward.

rows.text: Not in the sky, not in the midst of the sea, not if we enter into the clefts of the mountains, is there known a spot in the whole world where death could not overcome (the mortal).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: There is no place anywhere on earth, in the ocean, or in the mountains where a person can hide from death.
rows.riskNotes: The relentless universality of death stated here could intensulate raw grief by denying any imagined escape or sanctuary from loss.

rows.text: All men tremble at punishment, all men fear death; remember that you are like unto them, and do not kill, nor cause slaughter.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People naturally fear pain and death, so recognizing this shared vulnerability should lead one to refrain from killing or ordering killing.

rows.text: All men tremble at punishment, all men love life; remember that thou art like unto them, and do not kill, nor cause slaughter.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People share a universal aversion to suffering and a desire to survive; recognizing this commonality should lead one to refrain from killing or enabling killing.

rows.text: He who seeking his own happiness punishes or kills beings who also long for happiness, will not find happiness after death.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who harms or kills other beings in pursuit of their own happiness will not achieve happiness after death, since those other beings also desire happiness.
rows.riskNotes: The verse frames happiness as conditional on not harming others, which could intensify shame in someone already blaming themselves for past actions; the punitive framing and afterlife threat may also feel invalidating to someone processing relational harm they inflicted.

rows.text: He who seeking his own happiness does not punish or kill beings who also long for happiness, will find happiness after death.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who refrains from harming or killing other beings—who, like oneself, also want to be happy—because they want happiness themselves, will be happy after death.

rows.text: Do not speak harshly to anybody; those who are spoken to will answer thee in the same way. Angry speech is painful, blows for blows will touch thee.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Speaking harshly to others provokes the same response back. Hostile words cause suffering and lead to reciprocal harm.

rows.text: If, like a shattered metal plate (gong), thou utter not, then thou hast reached Nirvana; contention is not known to thee.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Complete inner silence—where one no longer reacts or speaks even when struck—eliminates conflict entirely and represents the highest freedom.

rows.text: As a cowherd with his staff drives his cows into the stable, so do Age and Death drive the life of men.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Time and mortality inevitably push all living beings toward their end, just as a herder guides cattle into an enclosure with no possibility of escape.
rows.riskNotes: The stark image of being driven like cattle toward death by implacable forces could intensulate raw grief by evoking helplessness and mortality at a time when someone needs gentler ground.

rows.text: A fool does not know when he commits his evil deeds: but the wicked man burns by his own deeds, as if burnt by fire.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who lacks wisdom fails to recognize their own harmful actions, yet suffers from them as if scorched by flames.
rows.riskNotes: The image of burning by one's own deeds and the label 'wicked man' can intensify self-condemnation in someone already struggling with guilt or shame about their actions.

rows.text: He who inflicts pain on innocent and harmless persons, will soon come to one of these ten states:
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who deliberately hurts those who have done no wrong and pose no threat will quickly face one of ten severe negative outcomes.
rows.riskNotes: For someone who has been harmed, the promise that their abuser will suffer can intensify obsessive rumination on vengeance or retraumatize by keeping focus on the aggressor's fate rather than their own healing.

rows.text: He will have cruel suffering, loss, injury of the body, heavy affliction, or loss of mind,
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who acts wrongly faces severe outcomes including physical harm, mental breakdown, or intense suffering.
rows.riskNotes: A stark litany of bodily and mental destruction can amplify catastrophic thinking in someone already fearing harm or loss, and offers no grounding or relief.

rows.text: Or a misfortune coming from the king, or a fearful accusation, or loss of relations, or destruction of treasures,
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: This verse lists severe external calamities that can befall a person as a result of hatred: royal punishment, false accusation, loss of loved ones, and destruction of wealth.
rows.riskNotes: Lists catastrophic losses in rapid succession—legal violence, family rupture, financial ruin—which can retraumatize someone already navigating recent loss or instability by vividly conjuring worst-case scenarios.

rows.text: Or lightning-fire will burn his houses; and when his body is destroyed, the fool will go to hell.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A foolish person's destructive actions lead to total ruin—material loss, bodily destruction, and afterlife suffering.
rows.riskNotes: Vivid imagery of violent destruction of home and body followed by damnation can intensify hopelessness or death-related distress in already fragile individuals.

rows.text: Not nakedness, not platted hair, not dirt, not fasting, or lying on the earth, not rubbing with dust, not sitting motionless, can purify a mortal who has not overcome desires.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: External religious practices like nudity, matted hair, dirt, fasting, or motionless meditation cannot cleanse someone who still harbors uncontrolled desires.
rows.riskNotes: The verse explicitly invalidates spiritual practices someone may have invested identity in, which could deepen shame for someone already feeling spiritually inadequate or like their efforts are worthless.

rows.text: He who, though dressed in fine apparel, exercises tranquillity, is quiet, subdued, restrained, chaste, and has ceased to find fault with all other beings, he indeed is a Brahmana, an ascetic (sramana), a friar (bhikshu).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: True religious worth comes from inner calm, self-control, sexual restraint, and refraining from criticizing others, not from wearing fine clothes.

rows.text: Is there in this world any man so restrained by humility that he does not mind reproof, as a well-trained horse the whip?
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The passage asks whether anyone exists who is so naturally humble and self-controlled that they accept criticism without resentment, comparing such a person to a well-trained horse that responds calmly to the whip.
rows.riskNotes: The comparison of a person to a horse accepting the whip reframes criticism and punishment as desirable, which could reinforce submission to mistreatment for those already prone to self-blame or in harmful relationships.

rows.text: Like a well-trained horse when touched by the whip, be ye active and lively, and by faith, by virtue, by energy, by meditation, by discernment of the law you will overcome this great pain (of reproof), perfect in knowledge and in behaviour, and never forgetful.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should respond to criticism with disciplined alertness, applying religious practice and moral development to endure the discomfort and emerge more fully developed in understanding and conduct.
rows.riskNotes: The whip metaphor and framing of suffering as something to be overcome through personal effort could reinforce shame in someone already feeling defective or not trying hard enough.

rows.text: Well-makers lead the water (wherever they like); fletchers bend the arrow; carpenters bend a log of wood; good people fashion themselves.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Skilled craftspeople actively shape raw materials to their purpose; similarly, good people deliberately cultivate and form their own character rather than leaving themselves unshaped by circumstance.
rows.riskNotes: The parallel structure implies suffering comes from not being 'good' enough to self-fashion, which can land as moral failure on someone already feeling defective or struggling with self-criticism.

rows.text: How is there laughter, how is there joy, as this world is always burning? Why do you not seek a light, ye who are surrounded by darkness?
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The passage questions how people can laugh and feel joy when existence is inherently unstable and suffering, urging those lost in ignorance to seek awakening instead of remaining complacent.
rows.riskNotes: The rhetorical question shames the listener's happiness as ignorance while they are 'surrounded by darkness,' which can deepen hopelessness in someone already feeling trapped by suffering or loss.

rows.text: Look at this dressed-up lump, covered with wounds, joined together, sickly, full of many thoughts, which has no strength, no hold!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The body is presented as a fragile, suffering construct—decorated externally but internally diseased, held together precariously, and dominated by restless thinking yet ultimately powerless and without secure grounding.
rows.riskNotes: The visceral disgust toward the body as a wounded, sickly lump can intensify self-loathing in someone already hating their own body or existence, and the graphic imagery of bodily decay may retraumatize someone recently bereaved or in acute grief.

rows.text: This body is wasted, full of sickness, and frail; this heap of corruption breaks to pieces, life indeed ends in death.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The physical body deteriorates through illness and fragility, eventually decomposing and dying; existence is inherently subject to decay and mortality.
rows.riskNotes: Graphic imagery of bodily decay and explicit statement that life ends in death could intensify despair or grief for someone recently bereaved or contemplating ending their life.

rows.text: Those white bones, like gourds thrown away in the autumn, what pleasure is there in looking at them?
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person questions what satisfaction anyone could find in gazing at bleached bones scattered like discarded gourds after harvest, pointing to the repulsive reality beneath surface appeal.
rows.riskNotes: Graphic imagery of discarded bones could intensify distress for someone recently bereaved or confronting mortality in raw form.

rows.text: After a stronghold has been made of the bones, it is covered with flesh and blood, and there dwell in it old age and death, pride and deceit.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The human body is a fortress built from bones, layered with flesh and blood, yet it houses inevitable decay, mortality, arrogance, and deception.
rows.riskNotes: The visceral imagery of bones, flesh, blood, and dwelling death could intensify distress for someone recently bereaved or facing recent loss, though it lacks accusatory force and functions more as sober anatomical reflection than threat.

rows.text: The brilliant chariots of kings are destroyed, the body also approaches destruction, but the virtue of good people never approaches destruction,—thus do the good say to the good.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Physical things decay and die, including royal power and human bodies, but moral excellence endures beyond destruction.
rows.riskNotes: The explicit mention of bodily destruction and decay could intensify pain for someone recently bereaved or facing recent loss, though the verse ultimately pivots to an affirming message about enduring virtue.

rows.text: A man who has learnt little, grows old like an ox; his flesh grows, but his knowledge does not grow.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who acquires little knowledge ages physically without growing mentally, accumulating years but not understanding.

rows.text: Looking for the maker of this tabernacle, I shall have to run through a course of many births, so long as I do not find (him); and painful is birth again and again. But now, maker of the tabernacle, thou hast been seen; thou shalt not make up this tabernacle again. All thy rafters are broken, thy ridge-pole is sundered; the mind, approaching the Eternal (visankhara, nirvana), has attained to the extinction of all desires.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The speaker describes a long search through repeated rebirths for whatever causes the self to be rebuilt, and declares that once this cause is seen, the structure collapses and the mind reaches a state beyond formation where wanting ends.
rows.riskNotes: The vivid imagery of breaking rafters and sundered ridge-pole, while metaphorical about spiritual liberation, could unsettle someone recently bereaved by evoking destruction of shelter and finality in bodily terms.

rows.text: Men who have not observed proper discipline, and have not gained treasure in their youth, perish like old herons in a lake without fish.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who fail to cultivate discipline and fail to build meaningful resources while young end up destitute and helpless in later life, like birds starving in an empty lake.
rows.riskNotes: The image of wasted youth and pathetic death in a barren lake can land as a harsh condemnation of one's entire life, especially for people already feeling they have failed or missed their chance.

rows.text: Men who have not observed proper discipline, and have not gained treasure in their youth, lie, like broken bows, sighing after the past.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who failed to practice discipline and failed to build something of value while they were young end up weakened and regretful in old age, lamenting wasted opportunities.
rows.riskNotes: The image of wasted youth and brokenness, with sighing regret, can land as a harsh personal indictment on someone already feeling they failed or missed their chance.

rows.text: If a man hold himself dear, let him watch himself carefully; during one at least out of the three watches a wise man should be watchful.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who values their own wellbeing should guard their behavior with sustained vigilance, maintaining awareness through all periods of the day rather than lapsing into carelessness.

rows.text: Let each man direct himself first to what is proper, then let him teach others; thus a wise man will not suffer.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should establish their own good conduct before attempting to instruct others; this ordering prevents personal suffering.
rows.riskNotes: The directive to 'direct himself first' before teaching others can land as a rebuke to someone already feeling inadequate or flawed, reinforcing the sense that their current state is the cause of their suffering.

rows.text: If a man make himself as he teaches others to be, then, being himself well subdued, he may subdue (others); one's own self is indeed difficult to subdue.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has actually done the hard work of controlling themselves gains the credibility and ability to guide others; self-discipline is harder than disciplining other people.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on self-subjugation and the difficulty of controlling oneself can reinforce feelings of inadequacy in those already struggling with self-criticism or believing they have failed to improve themselves.

rows.text: Self is the lord of self, who else could be the lord? With self well subdued, a man finds a lord such as few can find.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person is their own master; no one else can control them. Someone who successfully disciplines themselves achieves a rare and valuable kind of self-governance.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on self-mastery and subduing the self can land as a demand for self-control on someone already blaming themselves for their struggles, reinforcing the belief that their suffering stems from personal failure to govern themselves.

rows.text: The evil done by oneself, self-begotten, self-bred, crushes the foolish, as a diamond breaks a precious stone.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Self-created wrongdoing destroys the person who does it, with a force compared to a diamond shattering a gem.
rows.riskNotes: The verse frames suffering as entirely self-created and uses crushing imagery, which can intensify self-loathing in someone already blaming themselves for their pain.

rows.text: He whose wickedness is very great brings himself down to that state where his enemy wishes him to be, as a creeper does with the tree which it surrounds.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who commits serious wrongdoing ends up destroying themselves in exactly the way their enemies would want, just as a climbing vine strangles the tree it wraps around.
rows.riskNotes: The metaphor of a destructive creeper strangling a tree, combined with framing the person's suffering as caused by their own 'very great wickedness,' can deepen shame and hopelessness in someone already blaming themselves for their circumstances.

rows.text: Bad deeds, and deeds hurtful to ourselves, are easy to do; what is beneficial and good, that is very difficult to do.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Harmful actions come naturally and require little effort, while actions that are genuinely beneficial to oneself and others demand significant difficulty and discipline.
rows.riskNotes: For someone already struggling with guilt or self-criticism, the framing that bad deeds are easy while good is very difficult can reinforce feelings of personal failure and inadequacy.

rows.text: The foolish man who scorns the rule of the venerable (Arahat), of the elect (Ariya), of the virtuous, and follows false doctrine, he bears fruit to his own destruction, like the fruits of the Katthaka reed.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who rejects the guidance of accomplished and ethical teachers, choosing instead to follow false teachings, brings about their own ruin, just as certain reeds naturally produce destructive fruits.
rows.riskNotes: The verse condemns foolishness and false doctrine with imagery of self-destruction, which can intensify shame in someone already feeling defective or morally failed.

rows.text: By oneself the evil is done, by oneself one suffers; by oneself evil is left undone, by oneself one is purified. Purity and impurity belong to oneself, no one can purify another.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person is solely responsible for their own harmful actions and their own suffering, just as they are solely responsible for refraining from harm and achieving moral purification. No external agent can accomplish this inner transformation for someone else.
rows.riskNotes: The repeated emphasis on self-causation of suffering can reinforce shame in those already blaming themselves, and may invalidate survivors of abuse or relational harm by implying their suffering is self-caused rather than inflicted by others.

rows.text: Let no one forget his own duty for the sake of another's, however great; let a man, after he has discerned his own duty, be always attentive to his duty.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should not neglect their own responsibilities in order to serve someone else's, no matter how important that other person may be; once you understand what your own duty is, you should stay focused on it.

rows.text: Do not follow the evil law! Do not live on in thoughtlessness! Do not follow false doctrine! Be not a friend of the world.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The passage warns against four things: adopting harmful ethical frameworks, living without awareness, embracing teachings that misrepresent reality, and forming close bonds with worldly values and social conventions that distract from what matters.
rows.riskNotes: Four consecutive commands to stop being bad/wrong/thoughtless can feel like condemnation to someone already feeling flawed or hopeless, reinforcing a sense of fundamental inadequacy rather than offering a path forward.

rows.text: Rouse thyself! do not be idle! Follow the law of virtue! The virtuous rests in bliss in this world and in the next.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A call to active engagement with moral practice rather than laziness, promising that ethical effort yields wellbeing both now and after death.
rows.riskNotes: The command to 'rouse thyself' and 'do not be idle' can land as a harsh self-indictment for someone already feeling inadequate or exhausted, reinforcing their sense of personal failure rather than inspiring effort.

rows.text: Follow the law of virtue; do not follow that of sin. The virtuous rests in bliss in this world and in the next.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Living according to moral principles rather than wrongdoing leads to well-being both now and after death.

rows.text: Look upon the world as a bubble, look upon it as a mirage: the king of death does not see him who thus looks down upon the world.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Seeing through the world's apparent solidity—recognizing it as fragile and insubstantial—frees a person from the power of death.
rows.riskNotes: The imagery of death and the instruction to see life as illusory could feel invalidating to someone recently bereaved, for whom the world feels painfully real and attachment to the deceased is raw.

rows.text: Come, look at this glittering world, like unto a royal chariot; the foolish are immersed in it, but the wise do not touch it.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The world's attractions appear splendid and enticing, yet only those lacking discernment become entangled in them; those with clarity keep their distance.

rows.text: He who formerly was reckless and afterwards became sober, brightens up this world, like the moon when freed from clouds.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has abandoned reckless behavior and cultivated self-control becomes a source of clarity and inspiration to others, much as the moon becomes visible and radiant when clouds pass away.

rows.text: He whose evil deeds are covered by good deeds, brightens up this world, like the moon when freed from clouds.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person whose harmful actions are outweighed or concealed by beneficial actions becomes a positive presence, comparable to the moon becoming visible and radiant after clouds pass away.
rows.riskNotes: The framing that evil deeds must be covered or hidden by good deeds could reinforce shame cycles in those already harshly self-judging their past actions.

rows.text: This world is dark, few only can see here; a few only go to heaven, like birds escaped from the net.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Most people remain unaware and trapped in worldly existence, while only a rare few attain clarity and liberation.
rows.riskNotes: The image of darkness and rarity of escape could deepen hopelessness in someone already feeling trapped or pessimistic about their prospects.

rows.text: The swans go on the path of the sun, they go through the ether by means of their miraculous power; the wise are led out of this world, when they have conquered Mara and his train.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Advanced spiritual practitioners transcend ordinary existence through extraordinary abilities, ultimately overcoming death and its forces to leave the worldly realm behind.

rows.text: If a man has transgressed one law, and speaks lies, and scoffs at another world, there is no evil he will not do.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who breaks moral rules, lies, and denies any existence after death is capable of committing any wrongdoing, because these three faults remove the basic constraints that limit harmful behavior.
rows.riskNotes: The passage escalates a single moral failing into total moral corruption, which can devastate someone already struggling with shame or self-blame by confirming their worst fears about themselves as irredeemable.

rows.text: The uncharitable do not go to the world of the gods; fools only do not praise liberality; a wise man rejoices in liberality, and through it becomes blessed in the other world.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Stinginess prevents reaching favorable states after death, and foolish people fail to value giving, whereas wise people take joy in generosity and gain positive results from it.

rows.text: Better than sovereignty over the earth, better than going to heaven, better than lordship over all worlds, is the reward of the first step in holiness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The initial progress toward spiritual purity holds greater value than any worldly or celestial power, including universal dominion.

rows.text: He whose conquest is not conquered again, into whose conquest no one in this world enters, by what track can you lead him, the Awakened, the Omniscient, the trackless?
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person whose inner victory is complete and irreversible, whom no one can surpass or influence, follows no path that others could trace or guide. The awakened one has transcended all ordinary tracks of behavior and understanding.

rows.text: He whom no desire with its snares and poisons can lead astray, by what track can you lead him, the Awakened, the Omniscient, the trackless?
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has fully awakened and knows all things cannot be manipulated or misdirected by any enticement, because they have no vulnerabilities that others could exploit.

rows.text: Even the gods envy those who are awakened and not forgetful, who are given to meditation, who are wise, and who delight in the repose of retirement (from the world).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Even divine beings admire people who remain fully aware, practice meditation, cultivate understanding, and find satisfaction in solitude rather than worldly engagement.

rows.text: Difficult (to obtain) is the conception of men, difficult is the life of mortals, difficult is the hearing of the True Law, difficult is the birth of the Awakened (the attainment of Buddhahood).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Human existence itself is rare and precious, and encountering true teaching and achieving full awakening are even rarer opportunities that should not be wasted.

rows.text: Not to commit any sin, to do good, and to purify one's mind, that is the teaching of (all) the Awakened.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The core instruction attributed to enlightened beings is threefold: refrain from harmful actions, actively do beneficial deeds, and cultivate mental clarity.
rows.riskNotes: The imperative framing 'not to commit any sin' can land as moral perfectionism on someone already harshly self-critical, amplifying shame rather than offering guidance.

rows.text: The Awakened call patience the highest penance, long-suffering the highest Nirvana; for he is not an anchorite (pravragita) who strikes others, he is not an ascetic (sramana) who insults others.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The highest spiritual practice is described as patience and endurance rather than causing harm. True religious renunciation is defined by refraining from striking or insulting others, not by external markers of asceticism.
rows.riskNotes: The praise of patience and long-suffering as highest virtues could be weaponized against someone currently being harmed, pressuring them to endure mistreatment rather than protect themselves.

rows.text: Not to blame, not to strike, to live restrained under the law, to be moderate in eating, to sleep and sit alone, and to dwell on the highest thoughts,—this is the teaching of the Awakened.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The passage lists practical disciplines attributed to the Buddha: refraining from blaming or striking others, living within ethical boundaries, eating moderately, seeking solitude in rest and meditation, and focusing on elevated contemplation.
rows.riskNotes: The instruction 'not to strike' could implicitly pressure someone who has been struck to suppress justified anger or refrain from protective action, but the passage is framed as general monastic discipline rather than direct admonishment to a victim.

rows.text: There is no satisfying lusts, even by a shower of gold pieces; he who knows that lusts have a short taste and cause pain, he is wise;
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Material abundance cannot fulfill desire; someone who recognizes that pleasures are fleeting and bring suffering possesses true understanding.

rows.text: Even in heavenly pleasures he finds no satisfaction, the disciple who is fully awakened delights only in the destruction of all desires.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A fully awakened person does not find fulfillment even in the most refined pleasures; their only satisfaction comes from having eliminated all wanting.

rows.text: Men, driven by fear, go to many a refuge, to mountains and forests, to groves and sacred trees.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People seek safety in external places and objects when they feel afraid, rather than addressing fear itself.

rows.text: But that is not a safe refuge, that is not the best refuge; a man is not delivered from all pains after having gone to that refuge.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Ordinary refuges that people rely on fail to provide complete protection from suffering; true safety requires something more than conventional sources of comfort or escape.

rows.text: He who takes refuge with Buddha, the Law, and the Church; he who, with clear understanding, sees the four holy truths:—
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who seeks refuge in the Buddha, the teachings, and the monastic community, and who clearly comprehends the four noble truths.

rows.text: Viz. pain, the origin of pain, the destruction of pain, and the eightfold holy way that leads to the quieting of pain;—
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The passage enumerates the four noble truths: recognizing suffering, understanding its cause, knowing it can end, and following the eightfold path to achieve that cessation.

rows.text: That is the safe refuge, that is the best refuge; having gone to that refuge, a man is delivered from all pain.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Finding refuge in the Buddha's teaching offers complete liberation from suffering.

rows.text: A supernatural person (a Buddha) is not easily found, he is not born everywhere. Wherever such a sage is born, that race prospers.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A fully awakened person is rare and does not appear in every place or time. The community fortunate enough to have such a person among them flourishes as a result.

rows.text: Happy is the arising of the awakened, happy is the teaching of the True Law, happy is peace in the church, happy is the devotion of those who are at peace.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The verse lists four sources of genuine well-being: the presence of enlightened individuals, the availability of true teachings, harmony within the spiritual community, and the dedicated practice of those who have found inner calm.

rows.text: He who pays homage to those who deserve homage, whether the awakened (Buddha) or their disciples, those who have overcome the host (of evils), and crossed the flood of sorrow, he who pays homage to such as have found deliverance and know no fear, his merit can never be measured by anybody.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Honoring enlightened beings and those who have transcended suffering and fear generates immeasurable merit. The passage values recognizing and respecting those who have achieved spiritual liberation.

rows.text: Let us live happily then, not hating those who hate us! among men who hate us let us dwell free from hatred!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The passage advocates choosing to live without hatred even when surrounded by hostile people, making internal freedom from animosity the priority over external circumstances.
rows.riskNotes: The instruction to not hate those who hate us can feel like pressure to suppress justified anger for someone actively being harmed by others, though the overall framing is warm and inviting.

rows.text: Let us live happily then, free from ailments among the ailing! among men who are ailing let us dwell free from ailments!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The passage advocates for maintaining inner well-being and freedom from mental distress even when surrounded by others who suffer or are troubled.

rows.text: Let us live happily then, free from greed among the greedy! among men who are greedy let us dwell free from greed!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The passage advocates for maintaining a state of contentment and freedom from desire even when surrounded by people consumed by greed.

rows.text: Let us live happily then, though we call nothing our own! We shall be like the bright gods, feeding on happiness!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The passage advocates finding happiness through non-possessiveness rather than ownership, suggesting that freedom from attachment to possessions brings a joyful, almost divine state of being.

rows.text: Victory breeds hatred, for the conquered is unhappy. He who has given up both victory and defeat, he, the contented, is happy.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Winning creates resentment in the loser, making both parties miserable. True contentment comes from stepping outside the win-lose dynamic entirely.

rows.text: There is no fire like passion; there is no losing throw like hatred; there is no pain like this body; there is no happiness higher than rest.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Desire is the most destructive force, hatred the worst defeat, physical existence inherently painful, and tranquility the greatest possible satisfaction.

rows.text: Hunger is the worst of diseases, the body the greatest of pains; if one knows this truly, that is Nirvana, the highest happiness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Physical need and embodiment itself are identified as fundamental suffering; recognizing this truth is equated with liberation and ultimate peace.

rows.text: Health is the greatest of gifts, contentedness the best riches; trust is the best of relationships, Nirvana the highest happiness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The passage ranks what is most valuable in life: physical well-being, satisfaction with what one has, reliable relationships, and ultimately the liberation from suffering.

rows.text: He who has tasted the sweetness of solitude and tranquillity, is free from fear and free from sin, while he tastes the sweetness of drinking in the law.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who experiences the pleasure of being alone and calm, and who is without fear or moral fault, also finds pleasure in studying spiritual teachings.

rows.text: The sight of the elect (Arya) is good, to live with them is always happiness; if a man does not see fools, he will be truly happy.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Being around admirable people brings lasting satisfaction, while avoiding contact with unwise people contributes to genuine well-being.

rows.text: He who walks in the company of fools suffers a long way; company with fools, as with an enemy, is always painful; company with the wise is pleasure, like meeting with kinsfolk.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Associating with foolish people leads to prolonged difficulty and pain, similar to being with an enemy, while keeping company with wise people brings the comfort and ease of being with family.

rows.text: Therefore, one ought to follow the wise, the intelligent, the learned, the much enduring, the dutiful, the elect; one ought to follow a good and wise man, as the moon follows the path of the stars.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should seek out and follow those who are knowledgeable, patient, and virtuous, just as the moon moves along its course among the stars.

rows.text: He who gives himself to vanity, and does not give himself to meditation, forgetting the real aim (of life) and grasping at pleasure, will in time envy him who has exerted himself in meditation.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who pursues superficial distractions and sensory pleasure while neglecting contemplative practice will eventually feel regret and envy toward those who dedicated themselves to inner discipline.
rows.riskNotes: The verse frames spiritual failure as a personal choice to indulge in vanity and neglect effort, which could deepen shame for someone already feeling inadequate about their discipline or meditation practice.

rows.text: Let no man ever look for what is pleasant, or what is unpleasant. Not to see what is pleasant is pain, and it is pain to see what is unpleasant.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Seeking pleasant experiences and avoiding unpleasant ones both lead to suffering; the very act of pursuing or rejecting sensory experiences creates pain.

rows.text: Let, therefore, no man love anything; loss of the beloved is evil. Those who love nothing and hate nothing, have no fetters.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Emotional investment in people or things leads to suffering when they are lost. Freedom from both attraction and aversion brings liberation from bondage.
rows.riskNotes: The command to love nothing, framed as loss being evil, can land as emotional suppression or invalidation for someone mourning a recent loss; the stark renunciation may deepen numbness or despair in suicidal states.

rows.text: From pleasure comes grief, from pleasure comes fear; he who is free from pleasure knows neither grief nor fear.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Dependence on pleasure creates vulnerability to grief and fear; release from pleasure-dependence removes these emotional burdens.
rows.riskNotes: The framing of grief as originating from pleasure could invalidate someone's mourning by implying their loss was wrong to cherish, or that their love caused their pain.

rows.text: From affection comes grief, from affection comes fear; he who is free from affection knows neither grief nor fear.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Emotional bonds to people or things create vulnerability to loss and anxiety; releasing such attachment eliminates these forms of suffering.
rows.riskNotes: For someone actively grieving a lost loved one, the framing of affection as the root of suffering can invalidate their bond and pressure them to detach prematurely, compounding pain with guilt about their love.

rows.text: From lust comes grief, from lust comes fear; he who is free from lust knows neither grief nor fear.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Desire for pleasure produces suffering and anxiety; releasing that desire ends both.

rows.text: From love comes grief, from love comes fear; he who is free from love knows neither grief nor fear.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Emotional bonds to people or things create vulnerability to loss and anxiety; releasing such attachment eliminates these forms of suffering.
rows.riskNotes: For someone freshly grieving a lost loved one, the framing of love as the root of suffering can feel like invalidation of their bond and compound pain with spiritual blame.

rows.text: From greed comes grief, from greed comes fear; he who is free from greed knows neither grief nor fear.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Wanting things leads to suffering and anxiety; letting go of wanting brings relief and security.

rows.text: He who possesses virtue and intelligence, who is just, speaks the truth, and does what is his own business, him the world will hold dear.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who combines moral integrity with practical intelligence, acts fairly, tells the truth, and minds their own affairs will be valued by others.

rows.text: He in whom a desire for the Ineffable (Nirvana) has sprung up, who is satisfied in his mind, and whose thoughts are not bewildered by love, he is called urdhvamsrotas (carried upwards by the stream).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has developed longing for liberation, feels internally content, and is not confused by emotional entanglement is described as progressing upward spiritually.

rows.text: Kinsmen, friends, and lovers salute a man who has been long away, and returns safe from afar.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People naturally welcome and celebrate the return of someone who has been absent and arrives safely from a distant journey.
rows.riskNotes: The image of welcome homecoming can intensify loneliness or loss for someone freshly grieving a relationship that cannot be reunited, or who lacks such returning figures in their life.

rows.text: In like manner his good works receive him who has done good, and has gone from this world to the other;—as kinsmen receive a friend on his return.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Good deeds done in life welcome a person after death, just as family members welcome back a returning friend.
rows.riskNotes: The metaphor of being received after death and the mention of going to another world could intensify longing or pain for someone mourning a recent loss, though the overall tone is warm and consoling.

rows.text: Let a man leave anger, let him forsake pride, let him overcome all bondage! No sufferings befall the man who is not attached to name and form, and who calls nothing his own.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should abandon anger and pride, break free from all entanglements, and avoid identifying with name and form or claiming ownership over anything; such detachment prevents suffering.
rows.riskNotes: The command to 'leave anger' and 'forsake pride' can sound like invalidating righteous anger in someone processing relational harm, pressuring premature forgiveness or self-suppression rather than honoring their protective emotional response.

rows.text: He who holds back rising anger like a rolling chariot, him I call a real driver; other people are but holding the reins.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who can stop anger as it arises, like controlling a moving chariot, demonstrates true mastery; others merely go through the motions without real control.
rows.riskNotes: The metaphor of controlling anger as mastery may feel invalidating to someone actively processing justified rage at an abuser, implying their anger is mere lack of discipline rather than a legitimate response.

rows.text: Let a man overcome anger by love, let him overcome evil by good; let him overcome the greedy by liberality, the liar by truth!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The verse recommends responding to harmful behavior with its opposite: counter hostility with kindness, wickedness with goodness, greed with generosity, and dishonesty with truthfulness.
rows.riskNotes: The imperative to respond to harm with love and goodness can pressure someone processing relational trauma to suppress justified anger and prematurely forgive, replicating dynamics of enforced reconciliation.

rows.text: Speak the truth, do not yield to anger; give, if thou art asked for little; by these three steps thou wilt go near the gods.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Three practical behaviors—honesty, controlling anger, and generosity even with small things—are presented as a path to elevated spiritual states.
rows.riskNotes: The instruction to not yield to anger may feel invalidating to someone actively processing righteous anger at an aggressor, pressuring premature suppression rather than healthy acknowledgment.

rows.text: The sages who injure nobody, and who always control their body, they will go to the unchangeable place (Nirvana), where, if they have gone, they will suffer no more.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who harm no one and maintain disciplined physical behavior can reach a permanent state beyond suffering.

rows.text: Those who are ever watchful, who study day and night, and who strive after Nirvana, their passions will come to an end.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who maintain constant vigilance, dedicate themselves to continuous practice, and actively pursue liberation will see their desires and destructive emotions dissolve.

rows.text: This is an old saying, O Atula, this is not only of to-day: `They blame him who sits silent, they blame him who speaks much, they also blame him who says little; there is no one on earth who is not blamed.'
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Universal criticism is unavoidable regardless of how much or how little one speaks, or even if one remains silent.
rows.riskNotes: The universality of being blamed could reinforce shame for someone already feeling intensely criticized, making their self-condemnation feel validated rather than eased.

rows.text: There never was, there never will be, nor is there now, a man who is always blamed, or a man who is always praised.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Universal praise and universal blame are equally impossible; no person entirely escapes either.

rows.text: But he whom those who discriminate praise continually day after day, as without blemish, wise, rich in knowledge and virtue, who would dare to blame him, like a coin made of gold from the Gambu river? Even the gods praise him, he is praised even by Brahman.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person consistently praised by discerning people for being flawless, wise, and virtuous becomes beyond reproach, like pure gold; even divine beings honor such a person.

rows.text: Beware of bodily anger, and control thy body! Leave the sins of the body, and with thy body practise virtue!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Physical aggression and harmful bodily actions should be avoided; instead one should use the body to act morally.

rows.text: Beware of the anger of the tongue, and control thy tongue! Leave the sins of the tongue, and practise virtue with thy tongue!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The passage warns against harmful speech driven by anger and urges deliberate control of what one says, shifting from verbal harm to verbal goodness.

rows.text: Beware of the anger of the mind, and control thy mind! Leave the sins of the mind, and practise virtue with thy mind!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The passage warns against mental anger and urges the reader to abandon harmful mental states while cultivating virtuous ones through deliberate mental control.
rows.riskNotes: The repeated command to "control thy mind" and framing of mental states as "sins" can land as moral failure for someone already struggling with intrusive thoughts or emotional dysregulation, reinforcing shame rather than offering skillful means.

rows.text: The wise who control their body, who control their tongue, the wise who control their mind, are indeed well controlled.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who are wise exercise control over their physical actions, their speech, and their mental states, achieving genuine self-mastery through this comprehensive discipline.

rows.text: Thou art now like a sear leaf, the messengers of death (Yama) have come near to thee; thou standest at the door of thy departure, and thou hast no provision for thy journey.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person near death is compared to a withered leaf, unprepared and without provisions for what comes next, as the forces of mortality draw close.
rows.riskNotes: The visceral imagery of death's messengers arriving and being unprepared for departure could intensify mortality terror in someone already fearing death or recently bereaved, and the explicit 'no provision' framing may deepen hopelessness in those already despairing.

rows.text: Make thyself an island, work hard, be wise! When thy impurities are blown away, and thou art free from guilt, thou wilt enter into the heavenly world of the elect (Ariya).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should become self-reliant, apply themselves diligently, and cultivate understanding. Once moral faults are eliminated and wrongdoing is cleared, one attains the elevated state of the spiritually accomplished.
rows.riskNotes: The imperative to 'make thyself an island' and the focus on removing 'impurities' and 'guilt' can land as solitary self-reliance and personal defect for someone already feeling fundamentally flawed, amplifying shame rather than inspiring effort.

rows.text: Thy life has come to an end, thou art come near to death (Yama), there is no resting-place for thee on the road, and thou hast no provision for thy journey.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person facing death has arrived at the inevitable end with no refuge along the way and no preparation made for what comes after.
rows.riskNotes: Direct confrontation with mortality and death without consolation could intensify fear of dying or hopelessness in someone already facing loss or contemplating suicide.

rows.text: Make thyself an island, work hard, be wise! When thy impurities are blown away, and thou art free from guilt, thou wilt not enter again into birth and decay.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should become self-reliant and self-contained, put in serious effort, and develop understanding. Once inner defilements are removed and moral fault is eliminated, one escapes the cycle of repeated existence.
