rows.riskNotes: The imperative to work hard and the framing of impurities and guilt as personal obstacles could reinforce shame in someone already feeling fundamentally flawed or responsible for their suffering.

rows.text: Let a wise man blow off the impurities of his self, as a smith blows off the impurities of silver one by one, little by little, and from time to time.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should gradually and repeatedly remove their own moral flaws, working patiently over time like a metalworker refining silver by removing impurities bit by bit.
rows.riskNotes: The metaphor of 'impurities' needing to be laboriously removed can reinforce self-criticism in someone already convinced they are fundamentally flawed or dirty.

rows.text: As the impurity which springs from the iron, when it springs from it, destroys it; thus do a transgressor's own works lead him to the evil path.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person's own wrongful actions, like rust that corrodes iron from within, inevitably lead them toward harm and downfall.
rows.riskNotes: The metaphor of impurity destroying its source from within can intensify self-loathing in someone already convinced their own nature is fundamentally corrupt or that their suffering is self-caused.

rows.text: The taint of prayers is non-repetition; the taint of houses, non-repair; the taint of the body is sloth; the taint of a watchman, thoughtlessness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Neglect corrupts everything: prayers lose power when not repeated, houses decay without maintenance, bodies weaken through laziness, and watchmen fail through inattention. The verse treats vigilance and ongoing effort as necessary to prevent deterioration in every domain of life.

rows.text: Bad conduct is the taint of woman, greediness the taint of a benefactor; tainted are all evil ways in this world and in the next.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Certain roles carry characteristic moral failings—women through bad behavior, givers through greed—and all wrongful actions corrupt both this life and future ones.
rows.riskNotes: The verse uses loaded language about women being tainted by bad conduct, which can wound those already struggling with gender-based shame or internalized blame, and the broad condemnation of evil ways may reinforce self-blame in those already harshly judging themselves.

rows.text: But there is a taint worse than all taints,—ignorance is the greatest taint. O mendicants! throw off that taint, and become taintless!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The passage identifies ignorance as the most fundamental corruption to be eliminated in order to achieve a state of complete purity.
rows.riskNotes: The framing of ignorance as the greatest taint and urgent command to throw it off can land as moral failure for someone already feeling defective or not-enough, reinforcing shame rather than inspiring change.

rows.text: Life is easy to live for a man who is without shame, a crow hero, a mischief-maker, an insulting, bold, and wretched fellow.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who lacks shame and behaves aggressively—boastful, disruptive, insulting, and reckless—finds life easy because they ignore moral constraints that burden those with conscience.
rows.riskNotes: The passage heaps scorn on a contemptible person using loaded terms like shameless and wretched, which can reinforce harsh self-judgment in someone already struggling with shame or self-worth.

rows.text: But life is hard to live for a modest man, who always looks for what is pure, who is disinterested, quiet, spotless, and intelligent.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who maintains modesty, seeks moral purity, remains detached from worldly gain, lives quietly, keeps a clean reputation, and cultivates intelligence will find life difficult because such qualities are demanding to uphold in ordinary society.
rows.riskNotes: The idealized portrait of virtue may make someone already feeling inadequate or ashamed of their own perceived moral failures feel further diminished by comparison.

rows.text: He who destroys life, who speaks untruth, who in this world takes what is not given him, who goes to another man's wife;
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: This verse lists four harmful actions: killing, lying, stealing, and adultery. It identifies these as core moral violations in a straightforward catalog of wrong behavior.

rows.text: And the man who gives himself to drinking intoxicating liquors, he, even in this world, digs up his own root.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who habitually drinks alcohol undermines their own foundational well-being and stability, causing self-destruction that begins immediately rather than in some future life.
rows.riskNotes: The metaphor of digging up one's own root frames substance use as self-destruction, which could intensify shame in someone already struggling with addiction or recovery.

rows.text: O man, know this, that the unrestrained are in a bad state; take care that greediness and vice do not bring thee to grief for a long time!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A warning that lack of self-control leads to suffering, urging awareness that greed and misconduct cause lasting harm.
rows.riskNotes: The direct address to a morally failing person and warning of long-term grief from their own greediness and vice can intensify shame and self-recrimination in someone already struggling with those feelings.

rows.text: The world gives according to their faith or according to their pleasure: if a man frets about the food and the drink given to others, he will find no rest either by day or by night.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People receive things based on their own faith or preferences. Someone who obsesses over what others get will be perpetually restless.

rows.text: He in whom that feeling is destroyed, and taken out with the very root, finds rest by day and by night.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who has completely eliminated a destructive feeling, removing it entirely at its source, experiences calm continuously.

rows.text: There is no fire like passion, there is no shark like hatred, there is no snare like folly, there is no torrent like greed.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Passion, hatred, folly, and greed are compared to destructive forces—fire, a shark, a snare, and a torrent—emphasizing how each mental state traps and overwhelms a person.

rows.text: The fault of others is easily perceived, but that of oneself is difficult to perceive; a man winnows his neighbour's faults like chaff, but his own fault he hides, as a cheat hides the bad die from the gambler.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People readily notice and broadcast the flaws of others while concealing their own, much as a cheating gambler hides a loaded die.
rows.riskNotes: For someone already harshly self-critical, the metaphor of hiding one's own faults like a cheat can reinforce shame spirals by implying deliberate self-deception in their suffering.

rows.text: If a man looks after the faults of others, and is always inclined to be offended, his own passions will grow, and he is far from the destruction of passions.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Focusing on other people's flaws and taking offense easily strengthens one's own destructive mental states and prevents overcoming them.
rows.riskNotes: For someone already harshly self-critical, this can reinforce the pattern of self-monitoring for fault and turn inward as further evidence of personal failure.

rows.text: There is no path through the air, a man is not a Samana by outward acts. The world delights in vanity, the Tathagatas (the Buddhas) are free from vanity.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: External displays and surface behaviors do not constitute genuine spiritual attainment. Those who are truly awakened reject the empty appearances that ordinary people find appealing.

rows.text: There is no path through the air, a man is not a Samana by outward acts. No creatures are eternal; but the awakened (Buddha) are never shaken.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: External displays of religious practice do not make someone a true ascetic, and no being lasts forever, yet those who have achieved awakening remain unshaken by change.

rows.text: A man is not just if he carries a matter by violence; no, he who distinguishes both right and wrong, who is learned and leads others, not by violence, but by law and equity, and who is guarded by the law and intelligent, he is called just.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: True justice comes not from force but from understanding right and wrong, guiding others through fair principles rather than coercion, and living with intelligence under lawful restraint.

rows.text: A man is not learned because he talks much; he who is patient, free from hatred and fear, he is called learned.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: True learning is not measured by how much someone talks, but by their patience and freedom from hatred and fear.

rows.text: A man is not a supporter of the law because he talks much; even if a man has learnt little, but sees the law bodily, he is a supporter of the law, a man who never neglects the law.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Mastery of doctrine comes from embodied practice and consistent observance, not from extensive verbal knowledge or eloquent talk. Someone with modest learning who actually lives the teaching surpasses a verbose scholar who fails to apply it.

rows.text: A man is not an elder because his head is grey; his age may be ripe, but he is called `Old-in-vain.'
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Physical age and gray hair do not make someone truly mature or worthy of respect; mere seniority without wisdom or virtue is meaningless.

rows.text: He in whom there is truth, virtue, love, restraint, moderation, he who is free from impurity and is wise, he is called an elder.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who embodies honesty, moral integrity, loving-kindness, self-control, and balanced living, while also being free from defilements and possessing discernment, earns the respected title of elder.

rows.text: An envious greedy, dishonest man does not become respectable by means of much talking only, or by the beauty of his complexion.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who is envious, greedy, and dishonest cannot gain respect through eloquent speech or attractive appearance alone. External presentation does not compensate for flawed character.

rows.text: He in whom all this is destroyed, and taken out with the very root, he, when freed from hatred and wise, is called respectable.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has completely uprooted destructive qualities, and who is both free from hatred and wise, deserves genuine respect.

rows.text: Not by tonsure does an undisciplined man who speaks falsehood become a Samana; can a man be a Samana who is still held captive by desire and greediness?
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: External religious signs like shaved heads do not make someone a true ascetic if they still lie, lack discipline, and remain controlled by desire and greed. Genuine spiritual standing depends on inner conduct and freedom from craving, not outward appearance.

rows.text: He who always quiets the evil, whether small or large, he is called a Samana (a quiet man), because he has quieted all evil.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who consistently suppresses wrongdoing of any scale earns the title of one who has achieved stillness, because they have stilled all harmful impulses within themselves.

rows.text: A man is not a mendicant (Bhikshu) simply because he asks others for alms; he who adopts the whole law is a Bhikshu, not he who only begs.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: True religious mendicancy requires living by the complete moral code, not merely the external act of begging for food.

rows.text: He who is above good and evil, who is chaste, who with knowledge passes through the world, he indeed is called a Bhikshu.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A true renunciant is someone who lives beyond ordinary moral dualism, maintains purity, and moves through life with understanding rather than conventional judgment.

rows.text: A man is not a Muni because he observes silence (mona, i.e. mauna), if he is foolish and ignorant; but the wise who, taking the balance, chooses the good and avoids evil, he is a Muni, and is a Muni thereby; he who in this world weighs both sides is called a Muni.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: True wisdom is not found in outward silence or ascetic appearance but in the ability to carefully evaluate choices and deliberately pursue good while avoiding harm.

rows.text: A man is not an elect (Ariya) because he injures living creatures; because he has pity on all living creatures, therefore is a man called Ariya.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: True nobility comes from compassion toward all beings, not from causing harm. A person earns respect through kindness and the absence of cruelty.

rows.text: Not only by discipline and vows, not only by much learning, not by entering into a trance, not by sleeping alone, do I earn the happiness of release which no worldling can know. Bhikshu, be not confident as long as thou hast not attained the extinction of desires.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: External religious practices alone—discipline, study, meditation, solitude—do not bring liberation. True release requires eliminating desires entirely.
rows.riskNotes: The repeated negation of spiritual efforts and the final warning against confidence could land as invalidating for someone already feeling inadequate or harshly self-critical about their practice or progress.

rows.text: The best of ways is the eightfold; the best of truths the four words; the best of virtues passionlessness; the best of men he who has eyes to see.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The passage ranks spiritual attainments: the eightfold path as the supreme method, the four noble truths as the supreme teachings, freedom from craving as the supreme quality, and the person who perceives reality clearly as the supreme individual.

rows.text: This is the way, there is no other that leads to the purifying of intelligence. Go on this way! Everything else is the deceit of Mara (the tempter).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A single path exists for clearing the mind of delusion; all other routes are distractions that derail progress.

rows.text: If you go on this way, you will make an end of pain! The way was preached by me, when I had understood the removal of the thorns (in the flesh).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The speaker announces that following this path leads to the cessation of suffering, and that he taught this path after himself comprehending how to eliminate obstacles and afflictions.

rows.text: You yourself must make an effort. The Tathagatas (Buddhas) are only preachers. The thoughtful who enter the way are freed from the bondage of Mara.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Spiritual progress depends on one's own active striving rather than relying solely on teachers or external authorities. Those who thoughtfully commit to the path achieve liberation from destructive forces.
rows.riskNotes: The emphasis on personal effort alone can land as dismissive of systemic barriers or past trauma, and may reinforce shame in someone already feeling inadequate or blaming themselves for their struggles.

rows.text: `All created things perish,' he who knows and sees this becomes passive in pain; this is the way to purity.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Recognizing that everything that arises eventually ceases allows a person to remain unperturbed by suffering, which leads to liberation.
rows.riskNotes: The phrase 'all created things perish' could land as cold dismissal of attachment for someone actively mourning a recent loss, even though the verse aims at equanimity rather than cruelty.

rows.text: `All created things are grief and pain,' he who knows and sees this becomes passive in pain; this is the way that leads to purity.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Recognizing that all conditioned existence involves suffering allows a person to remain unmoved when pain arises; this understanding is a path to liberation.
rows.riskNotes: The declaration that all existence is grief and pain could deepen anguish for someone recently bereaved or freshly traumatized by loss, invalidating their specific sorrow by universalizing suffering rather than meeting it.

rows.text: `All forms are unreal,' he who knows and sees this becomes passive in pain; this is the way that leads to purity.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Recognizing that all phenomena lack permanent substance allows a person to remain unmoved by suffering, which constitutes a path toward clarity and freedom.

rows.text: He who does not rouse himself when it is time to rise, who, though young and strong, is full of sloth, whose will and thought are weak, that lazy and idle man will never find the way to knowledge.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who fails to act when action is needed, who remains sluggish despite having youth and strength, and whose intentions and mental focus are feeble, will not achieve understanding or progress.
rows.riskNotes: The passage's harsh characterization of laziness and weakness as moral failures could deepen shame for someone already struggling with low energy, depression, or executive dysfunction, making them feel fundamentally defective rather than merely encouraged.

rows.text: Watching his speech, well restrained in mind, let a man never commit any wrong with his body! Let a man but keep these three roads of action clear, and he will achieve the way which is taught by the wise.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who guards their speech, controls their mind, and avoids wrongdoing with their body will attain the path taught by the wise.

rows.text: Through zeal knowledge is gotten, through lack of zeal knowledge is lost; let a man who knows this double path of gain and loss thus place himself that knowledge may grow.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Active effort leads to understanding and growth, while laziness causes understanding to decline. A person aware of this dynamic should arrange their life to cultivate ongoing development.

rows.text: Cut down the whole forest (of lust), not a tree only! Danger comes out of the forest (of lust). When you have cut down both the forest (of lust) and its undergrowth, then, Bhikshus, you will be rid of the forest and free!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The passage urges complete elimination of sensual desire rather than partial or selective restraint, using the metaphor of clearing an entire forest rather than removing single trees. Total removal of lust and its secondary growths leads to liberation.

rows.text: So long as the love of man towards women, even the smallest, is not destroyed, so long is his mind in bondage, as the calf that drinks milk is to its mother.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Any remaining romantic or sexual attachment to women keeps a person's mind trapped and dependent, similar to how a nursing calf remains bound to its mother.

rows.text: Cut out the love of self, like an autumn lotus, with thy hand! Cherish the road of peace. Nirvana has been shown by Sugata (Buddha).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The passage instructs removing self-love as one would cut a lotus, and following the path of peace toward the goal the Buddha revealed.
rows.riskNotes: The command to cut out self-love can land as self-condemnation for someone already harshly self-critical, implying their suffering stems from a fundamental flaw they must aggressively excise.

rows.text: `Here I shall dwell in the rain, here in winter and summer,' thus the fool meditates, and does not think of his death.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person makes long-term plans for comfort and seasonal living without considering that death could intervene at any time.

rows.text: Death comes and carries off that man, praised for his children and flocks, his mind distracted, as a flood carries off a sleeping village.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who is absorbed in family and material prosperity, with attention scattered, is swept away by death as suddenly and completely as a flood overwhelms a sleeping village.
rows.riskNotes: The sudden, violent image of death sweeping away an unsuspecting person like a flood could retraumatize someone recently bereaved or amplify death fixation in suicidal ideation.

rows.text: Sons are no help, nor a father, nor relations; there is no help from kinsfolk for one whom death has seized.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Family bonds and relationships offer no protection or assistance when a person faces death.
rows.riskNotes: A person newly bereaved may experience this as invalidating the meaning of family bonds and intensifying isolation at a moment when connection matters most.

rows.text: A wise and good man who knows the meaning of this, should quickly clear the way that leads to Nirvana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who understands the path should promptly remove obstacles and begin practicing toward liberation.

rows.text: If by leaving a small pleasure one sees a great pleasure, let a wise man leave the small pleasure, and look to the great.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person of good judgment will forgo minor immediate gratification when they recognize it leads to substantially greater lasting benefit.

rows.text: He who, by causing pain to others, wishes to obtain pleasure for himself, he, entangled in the bonds of hatred, will never be free from hatred.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Someone who hurts others to benefit themselves becomes trapped in hatred and cannot escape it.

rows.text: What ought to be done is neglected, what ought not to be done is done; the desires of unruly, thoughtless people are always increasing.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who lack self-control and reflection fail to do what they should, do what they shouldn't, and find their wants constantly growing.
rows.riskNotes: Directly condemns neglect of duty and uncontrolled desire, which can land as personal accusation on someone already feeling inadequate or struggling with self-criticism.

rows.text: But they whose whole watchfulness is always directed to their body, who do not follow what ought not to be done, and who steadfastly do what ought to be done, the desires of such watchful and wise people will come to an end.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who maintain constant awareness of their physical conduct, avoid harmful actions, and consistently perform beneficial ones will see their cravings dissolve.

rows.text: A true Brahmana goes scatheless, though he have killed father and mother, and two valiant kings, though he has destroyed a kingdom with all its subjects.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A spiritually accomplished person remains unharmed by conventional moral judgments even when their actions appear to destroy family, authority, and social order, because they have transcended ordinary attachments and identifications.
rows.riskNotes: The verse's shocking framing of killing family and kings as going 'scatheless' for a 'true Brahmana' can sound like moral permission for violence or like spiritual bypassing of real harm, which could deeply confuse or retraumatize someone processing abuse or struggling with guilt over family conflict.

rows.text: A true Brahmana goes scatheless, though he have killed father and mother, and two holy kings, and an eminent man besides.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A spiritually accomplished person remains untroubled even when they have metaphorically eliminated the sources of worldly attachment and authority that bind ordinary people.
rows.riskNotes: The passage uses shocking familial violence as a metaphor for spiritual transcendence, which could retraumatize those who have experienced family harm or abuse by normalizing or trivializing such violence.

rows.text: The disciples of Gotama (Buddha) are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on Buddha.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Buddha's followers maintain constant alertness and keep their attention focused on the Buddha at all times.

rows.text: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on the law.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Followers of the Buddha maintain continuous alertness and keep their attention fixed on the teachings throughout all hours.

rows.text: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on the church.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Followers of Gotama maintain constant alertness and keep their attention continuously directed toward the spiritual community.

rows.text: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on their body.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Followers of the Buddha maintain constant alertness and keep their attention continuously focused on the physical body throughout all hours.

rows.text: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their mind day and night always delights in compassion.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Followers of the Buddha maintain constant alertness and find ongoing satisfaction in compassionate states of mind throughout all hours.

rows.text: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their mind day and night always delights in meditation.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Followers of the Buddha maintain constant alertness and find continuous satisfaction in meditative practice throughout all hours.

rows.text: It is hard to leave the world (to become a friar), it is hard to enjoy the world; hard is the monastery, painful are the houses; painful it is to dwell with equals (to share everything in common) and the itinerant mendicant is beset with pain. Therefore let no man be an itinerant mendicant and he will not be beset with pain.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The passage lists multiple forms of suffering: renouncing worldly life, remaining in it, monastic life, domestic life, communal living, and the wandering ascetic's existence. It concludes by advising against becoming a wandering mendicant to avoid that particular hardship.
rows.riskNotes: The relentless repetition of 'hard' and 'painful' with no relief or alternative offered could deepen hopelessness in someone already feeling trapped by life's difficulties, and the concluding advice to simply not become a mendicant may feel like a condemnation of spiritual seeking itself.

rows.text: Whatever place a faithful, virtuous, celebrated, and wealthy man chooses, there he is respected.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who possesses faith, moral virtue, good reputation, and material resources will be held in esteem regardless of where they go.

rows.text: Good people shine from afar, like the snowy mountains; bad people are not seen, like arrows shot by night.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People of good character are naturally visible and noticeable even at a distance, while people of bad character remain hidden and unnoticeable, like projectiles fired in darkness.
rows.riskNotes: The stark good/bad binary and visibility metaphor can intensify shame for someone already feeling fundamentally flawed or exposed as bad.

rows.text: He alone who, without ceasing, practises the duty of sitting alone and sleeping alone, he, subduing himself, will rejoice in the destruction of all desires alone, as if living in a forest.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who consistently practices solitude in sitting and sleeping, and who disciplines themselves, will find satisfaction in the complete elimination of desires, comparable to the freedom of living in a forest.

rows.text: He who says what is not, goes to hell; he also who, having done a thing, says I have not done it. After death both are equal, they are men with evil deeds in the next world.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who lie about facts and people who deny their own harmful actions face the same outcome after death; both carry their wrongdoing into whatever follows.
rows.riskNotes: The verse explicitly condemns liars and deniers to hell with equal evil destiny, which can intensify shame and self-recrimination in those already struggling with guilt or recent loss; the afterlife framing may also compound distress for those processing death.

rows.text: Many men whose shoulders are covered with the yellow gown are ill-conditioned and unrestrained; such evil-doers by their evil deeds go to hell.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who wear religious robes but behave badly and lack discipline will suffer negative consequences for their harmful actions.
rows.riskNotes: The passage condemns religious hypocrites as evil-doers destined for hell, which could intensify shame and self-condemnation in someone already struggling with feeling fundamentally bad or fraudulent in their spiritual practice.

rows.text: Better it would be to swallow a heated iron ball, like flaring fire, than that a bad unrestrained fellow should live on the charity of the land.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The verse states that enduring extreme physical pain would be preferable to living as an immoral person who survives on public charity without discipline.
rows.riskNotes: The graphic self-harm imagery and harsh condemnation of being unrestrained could devastate someone already feeling worthless or dependent on others' help.

rows.text: Four things does a wreckless man gain who covets his neighbour's wife,—a bad reputation, an uncomfortable bed, thirdly, punishment, and lastly, hell.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who pursues another's spouse through lust acquires four harmful outcomes: social disgrace, personal distress, legal or social punishment, and afterlife suffering.
rows.riskNotes: The passage uses threatening, punitive language about sexual transgression that could intensify shame in someone already struggling with guilt about relationships or fidelity, and the hell imagery may feel condemning rather than guiding.

rows.text: There is bad reputation, and the evil way (to hell), there is the short pleasure of the frightened in the arms of the frightened, and the king imposes heavy punishment; therefore let no man think of his neighbour's wife.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Sexual involvement with another person's spouse brings multiple harms: social disgrace, spiritual ruin, anxiety-ridden pleasure, and legal punishment. The passage advises against pursuing such relationships entirely.
rows.riskNotes: The passage threatens hell, punishment, and bad reputation as consequences of sexual misconduct, which could intensify shame and self-blame in someone already struggling with those feelings; the framing of desire as a fearful, guilty act may also feel coercive to someone processing relational harm or boundary violations.

rows.text: As a grass-blade, if badly grasped, cuts the arm, badly-practised asceticism leads to hell.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Practicing spiritual discipline in a misguided or careless way causes harm rather than benefit, just as holding a blade wrong causes injury instead of serving its purpose.
rows.riskNotes: The hell imagery and the framing of spiritual failure as self-inflicted harm could intensify fear and self-condemnation in someone already doubting their worth or practice.

rows.text: An act carelessly performed, a broken vow, and hesitating obedience to discipline, all this brings no great reward.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Half-hearted commitment to rules or practices yields little benefit. Incomplete dedication to what one has undertaken produces meager results.
rows.riskNotes: For someone already feeling defective or ashamed of their efforts, this verse's focus on careless acts and broken vows can amplify feelings of inadequacy about their own spiritual practice or life commitments.

rows.text: If anything is to be done, let a man do it, let him attack it vigorously! A careless pilgrim only scatters the dust of his passions more widely.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should commit fully to any task they undertake; half-hearted engagement merely spreads one's disruptive desires more broadly.
rows.riskNotes: The vigorous attack metaphor and condemnation of carelessness can land as a harsh self-indictment for someone already feeling inadequate or exhausted, implying their suffering stems from insufficient effort.

rows.text: An evil deed is better left undone, for a man repents of it afterwards; a good deed is better done, for having done it, one does not repent.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Doing harm leads to later regret, while acting well leaves no cause for remorse.
rows.riskNotes: The framing of evil deeds and repentance can amplify pre-existing shame by implying moral failure is the primary lens for evaluating past actions, which may deepen self-criticism in someone already struggling with guilt.

rows.text: Like a well-guarded frontier fort, with defences within and without, so let a man guard himself. Not a moment should escape, for they who allow the right moment to pass, suffer pain when they are in hell.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should maintain constant vigilance over their own mind and behavior, like a fortress with defenses on all sides. Those who fail to stay alert and let opportunities for self-control slip away will experience suffering as a result.
rows.riskNotes: The vivid hell-imagery and urgency of constant vigilance could intensify anxiety and self-criticism in someone already fearing they have failed or are irredeemable.

rows.text: They who are ashamed of what they ought not to be ashamed of, and are not ashamed of what they ought to be ashamed of, such men, embracing false doctrines enter the evil path.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who feel shame about harmless things while remaining unashamed of genuinely harmful behavior adopt wrong views and end up on a destructive course.
rows.riskNotes: This passage directly attacks people's sense of shame as misguided and wrong, which could deeply wound someone already struggling with shame or self-blame by invalidating their feelings and suggesting they are morally confused. The framing of 'ought' and 'ought not' regarding shame is particularly dangerous for those whose shame stems from trauma or abuse, as it mirrors gaslighting dynamics.

rows.text: They who fear when they ought not to fear, and fear not when they ought to fear, such men, embracing false doctrines, enter the evil path.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who are afraid of harmless things while remaining unafraid of genuine dangers, having adopted wrong beliefs, end up following a harmful course in life.
rows.riskNotes: The passage condemns people for having the wrong fears and embracing false doctrines, which could intensify shame in someone already doubting their own judgment or beliefs.

rows.text: They who forbid when there is nothing to be forbidden, and forbid not when there is something to be forbidden, such men, embracing false doctrines, enter the evil path.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who wrongly prohibit harmless things while failing to prohibit genuinely harmful things, having adopted false teachings, end up on a destructive path.
rows.riskNotes: The passage condemns people who get moral rules wrong as entering an evil path, which can intensify shame in someone already harshly self-judging their own moral failures or confused boundaries.

rows.text: They who know what is forbidden as forbidden, and what is not forbidden as not forbidden, such men, embracing the true doctrine, enter the good path.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who correctly distinguish prohibited actions from permitted ones, and follow this accurate understanding, live rightly.

rows.text: Silently shall I endure abuse as the elephant in battle endures the arrow sent from the bow: for the world is ill-natured.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person chooses to bear insults without retaliation, comparing this endurance to an elephant absorbing an arrow in combat, recognizing that people in general are hostile by nature.
rows.riskNotes: Explicitly commands silent endurance of abuse, which can reinforce learned helplessness in abuse survivors and shame those who cannot or should not endure mistreatment quietly.

rows.text: They lead a tamed elephant to battle, the king mounts a tamed elephant; the tamed is the best among men, he who silently endures abuse.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Trained and controlled beings are valued for difficult tasks; similarly, a person who remains composed and does not retaliate when insulted is considered superior to others.
rows.riskNotes: The praise of silently enduring abuse could reinforce staying in harmful situations for someone currently experiencing or recently disclosing abuse, implicitly valorizing passivity over safety-seeking.

rows.text: Mules are good, if tamed, and noble Sindhu horses, and elephants with large tusks; but he who tames himself is better still.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Domesticated animals like mules, horses, and elephants are valuable, but a person who has trained and controlled their own mind and behavior is more valuable still.

rows.text: For with these animals does no man reach the untrodden country (Nirvana), where a tamed man goes on a tamed animal, viz. on his own well-tamed self.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: External control over animals or other people does not lead to liberation; only mastery over one's own mind and impulses enables progress toward the ultimate goal.

rows.text: The elephant called Dhanapalaka, his temples running with sap, and difficult to hold, does not eat a morsel when bound; the elephant longs for the elephant grove.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A captured elephant in rut, despite its physical power, refuses food and pines for its forest home, illustrating how strong longing for a familiar place can override even basic needs.

rows.text: If a man becomes fat and a great eater, if he is sleepy and rolls himself about, that fool, like a hog fed on wash, is born again and again.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who overindulges in food, becomes lazy and physically inert, and lives without discipline will remain trapped in cycles of repeated existence, much like a pig that grows fat on filth and stays confined to its pen.
rows.riskNotes: The passage uses harsh, body-shaming language (fat, great eater, sleepy, rolls himself about, fool, hog) that directly attacks personal habits and appearance, which could deeply wound someone already struggling with self-worth or eating-related shame.

rows.text: This mind of mine went formerly wandering about as it liked, as it listed, as it pleased; but I shall now hold it in thoroughly, as the rider who holds the hook holds in the furious elephant.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The speaker describes how their mind used to wander without restraint, and declares their intention to bring it under firm control, comparing the effort to a rider using a hook to restrain a rampaging elephant.

rows.text: Be not thoughtless, watch your thoughts! Draw yourself out of the evil way, like an elephant sunk in mud.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person should stay alert and monitor their own thinking, actively pulling themselves away from harmful mental patterns with the same force an elephant uses to extract itself from deep mud.
rows.riskNotes: The command to watch thoughts and pull oneself from evil way can land as accusatory self-criticism for someone already harshly judging their own mind.

rows.text: If a man find a prudent companion who walks with him, is wise, and lives soberly, he may walk with him, overcoming all dangers, happy, but considerate.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who discovers a careful, intelligent, and temperate associate can journey alongside them, surmounting every peril with happiness and circumspection.

rows.text: If a man find no prudent companion who walks with him, is wise, and lives soberly, let him walk alone, like a king who has left his conquered country behind,—like an elephant in the forest.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When no suitable companion is available—someone prudent, wise, and temperate—it is better to live independently and contentedly, without the compromises of unsuitable association.

rows.text: It is better to live alone, there is no companionship with a fool; let a man walk alone, let him commit no sin, with few wishes, like an elephant in the forest.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Solitary living is preferable to keeping company with foolish people; one should live alone, avoid wrongdoing, and maintain modest desires, compared to an elephant wandering apart in the woods.
rows.riskNotes: The explicit rejection of companionship and equation of others with fools could reinforce isolation and self-blame in someone already hurt by relationships, though it does not directly shame or blame the reader for their suffering.

rows.text: If an occasion arises, friends are pleasant; enjoyment is pleasant, whatever be the cause; a good work is pleasant in the hour of death; the giving up of all grief is pleasant.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Four things bring satisfaction: friendship when needed, any source of pleasure, good deeds at life's end, and release from sorrow.
rows.riskNotes: The phrase 'the giving up of all grief is pleasant' could land as invalidating or pressure to release grief prematurely for someone actively mourning a recent loss.

rows.text: Pleasant in the world is the state of a mother, pleasant the state of a father, pleasant the state of a Samana, pleasant the state of a Brahmana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The verse lists four states or roles that bring pleasantness to the world: motherhood, fatherhood, and the religious lives of a wandering ascetic (Samana) and a learned spiritual teacher (Brahmana). It affirms the value of both family relationships and dedicated spiritual vocation.
rows.riskNotes: The repeated celebration of parental roles may intensify sorrow for someone recently bereaved of a parent or estranged from family.

rows.text: Pleasant is virtue lasting to old age, pleasant is a faith firmly rooted; pleasant is attainment of intelligence, pleasant is avoiding of sins.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Lasting moral integrity, stable conviction, developed understanding, and the absence of harmful actions are all described as sources of genuine satisfaction.

rows.text: The thirst of a thoughtless man grows like a creeper; he runs from life to life, like a monkey seeking fruit in the forest.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who lacks awareness lets desire expand uncontrollably, causing them to cycle repeatedly through existence in restless pursuit of satisfaction, never stopping to recognize the futility of the chase.

rows.text: Whomsoever this fierce thirst overcomes, full of poison, in this world, his sufferings increase like the abounding Birana grass.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: When a person is overwhelmed by intense desire, their suffering multiplies and spreads uncontrollably, like invasive weeds taking over a field.
rows.riskNotes: The metaphor of poison and escalating suffering could intensify hopelessness in someone already feeling trapped by their own pain or cravings, implying an unstoppable worsening rather than offering a path out.

rows.text: He who overcomes this fierce thirst, difficult to be conquered in this world, sufferings fall off from him, like water-drops from a lotus leaf.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who conquers intense, hard-to-overcome desire finds that suffering falls away naturally, the way water slides off a lotus leaf without sticking.

rows.text: This salutary word I tell you, `Do ye, as many as are here assembled, dig up the root of thirst, as he who wants the sweet-scented Usira root must dig up the Birana grass, that Mara (the tempter) may not crush you again and again, as the stream crushes the reeds.'
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The speaker urges an assembled group to completely uproot their desire, using the analogy of digging up grass to reach a fragrant root, so that the tempter cannot repeatedly overpower them as a stream overwhelms reeds.

rows.text: As a tree, even though it has been cut down, is firm so long as its root is safe, and grows again, thus, unless the feeders of thirst are destroyed, the pain (of life) will return again and again.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Suffering keeps reviving as long as the underlying roots of desire remain intact, just as a tree regrows from its surviving root.

rows.text: He whose thirst running towards pleasure is exceeding strong in the thirty-six channels, the waves will carry away that misguided man, viz. his desires which are set on passion.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person whose intense craving for pleasure spreads through many pathways will be swept away by desire, because their wants are fixated on sensual passion.
rows.riskNotes: The metaphor of being carried away by waves and labeled misguided could reinforce shame in someone already struggling with compulsive behavior or addiction, implying moral failure rather than offering compassion.

rows.text: The channels run everywhere, the creeper (of passion) stands sprouting; if you see the creeper springing up, cut its root by means of knowledge.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Desire spreads through many pathways like a growing vine; when you notice it emerging, use understanding to destroy it at its source.

rows.text: A creature's pleasures are extravagant and luxurious; sunk in lust and looking for pleasure, men undergo (again and again) birth and decay.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who chase intense sensory pleasure and remain trapped in desire keep cycling through repeated birth and decay.

rows.text: Men, driven on by thirst, run about like a snared hare; held in fetters and bonds, they undergo pain for a long time, again and again.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People controlled by desire keep rushing restlessly, trapped in repetitive suffering they cannot escape, like animals caught in a snare.
rows.riskNotes: The image of being trapped and suffering repeatedly could intensify feelings of entrapment and hopelessness in someone already feeling stuck in pain.

rows.text: Men, driven on by thirst, run about like a snared hare; let therefore the mendicant drive out thirst, by striving after passionlessness for himself.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People chase desires frantically, trapped in repetitive suffering like a caught animal. A serious practitioner should actively work to eliminate this compulsive wanting by cultivating freedom from passion.

rows.text: He who having got rid of the forest (of lust) (i.e. after having reached Nirvana) gives himself over to forest-life (i.e. to lust), and who, when removed from the forest (i.e. from lust), runs to the forest (i.e. to lust), look at that man! though free, he runs into bondage.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who escapes desire but then returns to it, or who leaves desire only to pursue it again, is voluntarily choosing imprisonment despite having known freedom.

rows.text: Wise people do not call that a strong fetter which is made of iron, wood, or hemp; far stronger is the care for precious stones and rings, for sons and a wife.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The deepest bonds are not physical restraints but emotional entanglements with family and possessions, which wise people recognize as far more powerful.

rows.text: That fetter wise people call strong which drags down, yields, but is difficult to undo; after having cut this at last, people leave the world, free from cares, and leaving desires and pleasures behind.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The strongest bond is the one that pulls a person downward while seeming to give way, making it hard to break; only by finally severing it can someone depart from worldly life unburdened and without longing.

rows.text: Those who are slaves to passions, run down with the stream (of desires), as a spider runs down the web which he has made himself; when they have cut this, at last, wise people leave the world free from cares, leaving all affection behind.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People controlled by their desires become trapped in patterns they themselves created, like a spider caught in its own web. Those who break free from these self-made bonds can leave behind worldly attachments and live without anxiety.

rows.text: Give up what is before, give up what is behind, give up what is in the middle, when thou goest to the other shore of existence; if thy mind is altogether free, thou wilt not again enter into birth and decay.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: To reach liberation, one must let go of past, future, and present attachments; complete mental freedom ends the cycle of rebirth and death.
rows.riskNotes: The repeated command to 'give up' combined with imagery of crossing to 'the other shore' and escaping 'birth and decay' could feel dismissive to someone mourning a recent death, pressuring them to renounce attachment before they are ready.

rows.text: If a man is tossed about by doubts, full of strong passions, and yearning only for what is delightful, his thirst will grow more and more, and he will indeed make his fetters strong.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who is indecisive, driven by intense desires, and fixated on pleasure will find their craving intensifying and their own bondage strengthening.
rows.riskNotes: The passage frames internal struggle as self-inflicted bondage, which could deepen shame in someone already blaming themselves for their emotional state; the causal framing that one's own desires and doubts actively strengthen suffering may feel like condemnation to someone in despair.

rows.text: If a man delights in quieting doubts, and, always reflecting, dwells on what is not delightful (the impurity of the body, &c.), he certainly will remove, nay, he will cut the fetter of Mara.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who actively works to settle their uncertainties and deliberately contemplates what is naturally unappealing—such as the body's imperfection—will weaken and ultimately sever the bonds that keep them trapped in cyclic suffering.

rows.text: He who has reached the consummation, who does not tremble, who is without thirst and without sin, he has broken all the thorns of life: this will be his last body.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has achieved complete spiritual fulfillment, free from fear, desire, and moral fault, has overcome all life's hardships and will not be reborn again.
rows.riskNotes: The phrase 'last body' and imagery of finality around death could land heavily on someone recently bereaved, evoking painful thoughts of permanent loss rather than liberation.

rows.text: He who is without thirst and without affection, who understands the words and their interpretation, who knows the order of letters (those which are before and which are after), he has received his last body, he is called the great sage, the great man.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has eliminated desire and clinging, who comprehends language and meaning in full detail, has reached the final stage of existence and is recognized as an accomplished sage.

rows.text: `I have conquered all, I know all, in all conditions of life I am free from taint; I have left all, and through the destruction of thirst I am free; having learnt myself, whom shall I teach?'
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A speaker claims complete spiritual attainment—total knowledge, freedom from all defilement, and liberation through ending craving—yet this very declaration of having nothing left to learn or teach reveals a subtle arrogance that undermines the claim.

rows.text: The gift of the law exceeds all gifts; the sweetness of the law exceeds all sweetness; the delight in the law exceeds all delights; the extinction of thirst overcomes all pain.
