rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Spiritual teachings and liberation from desire are presented as surpassing all material pleasures and worldly satisfactions, with ending craving described as the ultimate relief from suffering.

rows.text: Pleasures destroy the foolish, if they look not for the other shore; the foolish by his thirst for pleasures destroys himself, as if he were his own enemy.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: People who lack foresight ruin themselves by pursuing immediate gratification; their own desire for pleasure becomes the instrument of their downfall.
rows.riskNotes: The repeated label 'the foolish' and framing of self-destruction as self-enmity can land as moral condemnation on someone already feeling defective or responsible for their own suffering.

rows.text: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by passion: therefore a gift bestowed on the passionless brings great reward.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as weeds ruin crops, destructive desires harm people; giving to those who have freed themselves from such passions yields especially valuable results.

rows.text: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by hatred: therefore a gift bestowed on those who do not hate brings great reward.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as weeds ruin crops, hatred harms people. Giving to those free from hatred yields significant benefit.

rows.text: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by vanity: therefore a gift bestowed on those who are free from vanity brings great reward.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as weeds ruin crops, vanity harms people. Giving to those without conceit yields significant benefit.

rows.text: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by lust: therefore a gift bestowed on those who are free from lust brings great reward.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as weeds ruin crops, desire harms people. Giving to those who have overcome desire yields especially good results.

rows.text: Restraint in the eye is good, good is restraint in the ear, in the nose restraint is good, good is restraint in the tongue.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Controlling the senses—sight, hearing, smell, and taste—is described as beneficial.

rows.text: In the body restraint is good, good is restraint in speech, in thought restraint is good, good is restraint in all things. A Bhikshu, restrained in all things, is freed from all pain.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Restraint in physical action, speech, and thought is presented as universally beneficial. Someone who practices complete self-restraint achieves liberation from suffering.

rows.text: He who controls his hand, he who controls his feet, he who controls his speech, he who is well controlled, he who delights inwardly, who is collected, who is solitary and content, him they call Bhikshu.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A mendicant is defined by mastery over body and speech, inner composure, solitude, and contentment rather than by external appearance or social role.

rows.text: The Bhikshu who controls his mouth, who speaks wisely and calmly, who teaches the meaning and the law, his word is sweet.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A mendicant who governs what he says, speaks with intelligence and composure, and explains teachings and principles will have speech that others find pleasant and welcome.

rows.text: He who dwells in the law, delights in the law, meditates on the law, follows the law, that Bhikshu will never fall away from the true law.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A mendicant who consistently lives within, takes pleasure in, reflects on, and adheres to moral teachings will remain firmly established in those teachings.

rows.text: Let him not despise what he has received, nor ever envy others: a mendicant who envies others does not obtain peace of mind.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person on a spiritual path should neither undervalue their own circumstances nor desire what others have, because wanting what belongs to others prevents inner calm.

rows.text: A Bhikshu who, though he receives little, does not despise what he has received, even the gods will praise him, if his life is pure, and if he is not slothful.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A religious wanderer who remains uncomplaining about small offerings, keeps ethical conduct clean, and stays energetically active earns even divine respect.

rows.text: He who never identifies himself with name and form, and does not grieve over what is no more, he indeed is called a Bhikshu.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A true mendicant is someone who does not cling to identity or material existence, and who remains unburdened by loss of what has passed away.
rows.riskNotes: The phrase 'does not grieve over what is no more' could land as invalidating or shaming for someone actively mourning a recent loss, implying they should already be beyond grief.

rows.text: The Bhikshu who acts with kindness, who is calm in the doctrine of Buddha, will reach the quiet place (Nirvana), cessation of natural desires, and happiness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A mendicant who behaves kindly and remains composed within Buddhist teaching will attain Nirvana, which is described as a state where natural desires end and happiness is found.

rows.text: O Bhikshu, empty this boat! if emptied, it will go quickly; having cut off passion and hatred thou wilt go to Nirvana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A mendicant is told to clear out passion and hatred, since shedding these burdens allows swifter progress toward liberation.

rows.text: Cut off the five (senses), leave the five, rise above the five. A Bhikshu, who has escaped from the five fetters, he is called Oghatinna, `saved from the flood.'
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A mendicant achieves liberation by transcending the five senses, abandoning the five hindrances, and overcoming the five lower fetters; such complete escape from sensory bondage constitutes true salvation.

rows.text: Meditate, O Bhikshu, and be not heedless! Do not direct thy thought to what gives pleasure that thou mayest not for thy heedlessness have to swallow the iron ball (in hell), and that thou mayest not cry out when burning, `This is pain.'
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A mendicant is urged to maintain meditation practice and avoid carelessness. The passage warns that indulging in pleasurable distractions leads to severe suffering, depicted through imagery of an iron ball and burning.
rows.riskNotes: Vivid hell imagery and explicit threat of physical torture for heedlessness could intensify fear, self-punishment, and catastrophic thinking in those already struggling with anxiety, shame, or suicidal states.

rows.text: Without knowledge there is no meditation, without meditation there is no knowledge: he who has knowledge and meditation is near unto Nirvana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Knowledge and meditation depend on each other; together they bring one close to liberation.

rows.text: A Bhikshu who has entered his empty house, and whose mind is tranquil, feels a more than human delight when he sees the law clearly.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A renunciant who dwells in solitude with a settled mind experiences extraordinary joy upon gaining clear insight into truth.

rows.text: As soon as he has considered the origin and destruction of the elements (khandha) of the body, he finds happiness and joy which belong to those who know the immortal (Nirvana).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who reflects on how the components of the body arise and pass away discovers a lasting happiness and joy known by those who understand liberation.

rows.text: And this is the beginning here for a wise Bhikshu: watchfulness over the senses, contentedness, restraint under the law; keep noble friends whose life is pure, and who are not slothful.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A wise mendicant starts with guarding the senses, being content, following discipline, and choosing diligent companions who live ethically.

rows.text: Let him live in charity, let him be perfect in his duties; then in the fulness of delight he will make an end of suffering.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who lives with kindness and fulfills their responsibilities fully will find deep satisfaction and overcome suffering.

rows.text: As the Vassika plant sheds its withered flowers, men should shed passion and hatred, O ye Bhikshus!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Just as a plant naturally drops its dead blossoms, people should deliberately let go of passionate desire and hostile feelings.

rows.text: The Bhikshu whose body and tongue and mind are quieted, who is collected, and has rejected the baits of the world, he is called quiet.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A mendicant who has calmed physical, verbal, and mental activity, who is composed and has abandoned worldly temptations, is regarded as truly tranquil.

rows.text: Rouse thyself by thyself, examine thyself by thyself, thus self-protected and attentive wilt thou live happily, O Bhikshu!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person must motivate and scrutinize themselves rather than relying on external sources; through this self-guarded vigilance, one achieves wellbeing.
rows.riskNotes: The repeated emphasis on self-reliance and self-examination can land as blame for someone already feeling defective or exhausted, implying their unhappiness stems from insufficient self-effort.

rows.text: For self is the lord of self, self is the refuge of self; therefore curb thyself as the merchant curbs a good horse.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person must govern and rely upon themselves, exercising disciplined control over their own mind and behavior as a skilled handler manages a well-trained horse.
rows.riskNotes: The repeated emphasis on self as sole lord and refuge, combined with the imperative to 'curb thyself,' can land as blaming for those already feeling they have failed to control their suffering or circumstances.

rows.text: The Bhikshu, full of delight, who is calm in the doctrine of Buddha will reach the quiet place (Nirvana), cessation of natural desires, and happiness.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A mendicant who finds joy and tranquility through the Buddha's teaching will attain Nirvana, where cravings end and lasting happiness exists.

rows.text: He who, even as a young Bhikshu, applies himself to the doctrine of Buddha, brightens up this world, like the moon when free from clouds.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A young monk who earnestly practices Buddhist teaching brings light to the world, compared to the moon appearing bright when unobscured by clouds.

rows.text: Stop the stream valiantly, drive away the desires, O Brahmana! When you have understood the destruction of all that was made, you will understand that which was not made.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person of spiritual training must actively resist the pull of desires and recognize that all constructed things pass away; through this understanding, they can come to know the unconditioned.

rows.text: If the Brahmana has reached the other shore in both laws (in restraint and contemplation), all bonds vanish from him who has obtained knowledge.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has fully mastered both ethical discipline and meditative practice becomes completely free, as all ties dissolve through deep understanding.

rows.text: He for whom there is neither this nor that shore, nor both, him, the fearless and unshackled, I call indeed a Brahmana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The passage describes someone who has transcended all dualistic boundaries—neither this side nor the other, nor both together—and who lives without fear or attachment, calling such a person truly accomplished.

rows.text: He who is thoughtful, blameless, settled, dutiful, without passions, and who has attained the highest end, him I call indeed a Brahmana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who is reflective, morally faultless, stable, responsible, and free from desires—having reached the ultimate goal—fits the true ideal of spiritual excellence.

rows.text: The sun is bright by day, the moon shines by night, the warrior is bright in his armour, the Brahmana is bright in his meditation; but Buddha, the Awakened, is bright with splendour day and night.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The passage compares various sources of brightness or excellence—sun, moon, warrior, meditating Brahmana—and states that the Buddha surpasses them all by being radiant at all times.

rows.text: Because a man is rid of evil, therefore he is called Brahmana; because he walks quietly, therefore he is called Samana; because he has sent away his own impurities, therefore he is called Pravragita (Pabbagita, a pilgrim).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person earns the titles of spiritual attainment—Brahmana, Samana, or renunciant—not from birth or external identity, but from actual elimination of wrongdoing, calm conduct, and personal purification.

rows.text: No one should attack a Brahmana, but no Brahmana (if attacked) should let himself fly at his aggressor! Woe to him who strikes a Brahmana, more woe to him who flies at his aggressor!
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The passage states that attacking a spiritually developed person is harmful, but responding with retaliation is even worse. It condemns both the initial aggression and the reactive violence that follows.
rows.riskNotes: The verse condemns both aggression and self-defense with escalating woe, which can shame someone who defended themselves against harm and invalidate protective anger in abuse survivors.

rows.text: It advantages a Brahmana not a little if he holds his mind back from the pleasures of life; when all wish to injure has vanished, pain will cease.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A spiritually developed person benefits significantly from restraining their mind from worldly pleasures; when the desire to harm others disappears, suffering ends.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who does not offend by body, word, or thought, and is controlled on these three points.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person worthy of highest respect maintains discipline across all three channels of action: physical behavior, spoken words, and mental intentions.

rows.text: After a man has once understood the law as taught by the Well-awakened (Buddha), let him worship it carefully, as the Brahmana worships the sacrificial fire.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has comprehended the Buddha's teaching should devote careful, reverent attention to practicing it, comparable to how a priest tends a sacred fire with diligence and respect.

rows.text: A man does not become a Brahmana by his platted hair, by his family, or by birth; in whom there is truth and righteousness, he is blessed, he is a Brahmana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: Social status, religious appearance, and birth do not determine spiritual worth. Only truthfulness and righteous conduct define who is genuinely noble.

rows.text: What is the use of platted hair, O fool! what of the raiment of goat-skins? Within thee there is ravening, but the outside thou makest clean.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: External religious practices like matted hair and animal-skin clothing are pointless when someone harbors inner greed or desire. The verse criticizes those who perform outward purity while remaining spiritually unclean inside.
rows.riskNotes: The direct address "O fool!" and the accusation of hypocrisy could land as personal attack on someone already feeling defective or fraudulent, reinforcing shame about perceived inner inadequacy versus external presentation.

rows.text: The man who wears dirty raiments, who is emaciated and covered with veins, who lives alone in the forest, and meditates, him I call indeed a Brahmana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who lives in solitude, wears worn clothes, becomes physically thin from spiritual practice, and devotes themselves to meditation is praised as a true Brahmana.

rows.text: I do not call a man a Brahmana because of his origin or of his mother. He is indeed arrogant, and he is wealthy: but the poor, who is free from all attachments, him I call indeed a Brahmana.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: True spiritual worth comes from freedom from attachments, not from birth status or wealth. A genuinely admirable person is the one who lacks clinging, regardless of poverty or humble origins.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has cut all fetters, who never trembles, is independent and unshackled.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has severed all bonds, who remains unshaken, self-reliant, and completely free is the true exemplar.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has cut the strap and the thong, the chain with all that pertains to it, who has burst the bar, and is awakened.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A true spiritual adept is someone who has severed all bonds and constraints, both external and internal, and has achieved full awakening.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, though he has committed no offence, endures reproach, bonds, and stripes, who has endurance for his force, and strength for his army.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who remains patient and unprovoked even when falsely accused, punished, or physically harmed is praised; their true power lies in forbearance rather than retaliation.
rows.riskNotes: The verse valorizes passive endurance of abuse as spiritual virtue, which could reinforce learned helplessness in someone already being harmed or blamed, and may shame those who cannot endure mistreatment.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who is free from anger, dutiful, virtuous, without appetite, who is subdued, and has received his last body.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: The passage defines an ideal spiritual person as someone who has eliminated anger, fulfills obligations, lives ethically, lacks craving, controls themselves, and will not be reborn again.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who does not cling to pleasures, like water on a lotus leaf, like a mustard seed on the point of a needle.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A truly accomplished person remains untouched by sensory pleasures, maintaining complete non-attachment to worldly delights.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, even here, knows the end of his suffering, has put down his burden, and is unshackled.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A true spiritual adept is someone who comprehends the cessation of their own suffering in this life, has released their worldly obligations, and lives free from all bonds.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana whose knowledge is deep, who possesses wisdom, who knows the right way and the wrong, and has attained the highest end.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A true spiritual elite is someone whose understanding runs deep, who can discern correct from incorrect paths, and who has reached the ultimate goal.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who keeps aloof both from laymen and from mendicants, who frequents no houses, and has but few desires.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A true spiritual practitioner is defined by deliberate separation from social entanglements, minimal contact with households, and the reduction of wants rather than their fulfillment.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who finds no fault with other beings, whether feeble or strong, and does not kill nor cause slaughter.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person worthy of highest respect refrains from criticizing others regardless of their power or weakness, and abstains from killing or enabling killing.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who is tolerant with the intolerant, mild with fault-finders, and free from passion among the passionate.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person worthy of highest respect remains calm and unprovoked when others are hostile, responds gently to those who criticize, and stays free of craving even surrounded by people driven by desire.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of tolerance toward the intolerant and mildness toward fault-finders can be weaponized against someone currently being harmed, pressuring them to suppress legitimate anger or accept mistreatment as spiritual practice.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana from whom anger and hatred, pride and envy have dropt like a mustard seed from the point of a needle.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has completely shed anger, hatred, pride, and envy—letting them fall away as easily as a tiny seed slips from a needle's tip—is truly worthy of highest respect.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who utters true speech, instructive and free from harshness, so that he offend no one.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person worthy of highest respect speaks truthfully in ways that teach others while deliberately avoiding language that would cause harm or offense to anyone.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who takes nothing in the world that is not given him, be it long or short, small or large, good or bad.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person worthy of highest respect is one who never takes anything without permission, regardless of the object's size, shape, or value.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who fosters no desires for this world or for the next, has no inclinations, and is unshackled.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A true spiritual person is defined as someone who wants nothing from any realm of existence, holds no preferences, and is completely free from bonds.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has no interests, and when he has understood (the truth), does not say How, how? and who has reached the depth of the Immortal.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A true spiritual person is one who has no personal stakes or investments, who does not react with anxious questioning upon understanding reality, and who has fully realized the deathless state.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who in this world is above good and evil, above the bondage of both, free from grief from sin, and from impurity.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has transcended ordinary moral categories and their psychological constraints—neither bound by virtue nor vice, and free from emotional suffering and moral contamination—is the true exemplar.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who is bright like the moon, pure, serene, undisturbed, and in whom all gaiety is extinct.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A true spiritual person is described as luminous, pure, calm, and free from ordinary excitement or pleasure-seeking.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has traversed this miry road, the impassable world and its vanity, who has gone through, and reached the other shore, is thoughtful, guileless, free from doubts, free from attachment, and content.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has navigated through life's difficulties and deceptions, emerged with clarity, and remains without deceit, uncertainty, clinging, or discontent.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who in this world, leaving all desires, travels about without a home, and in whom all concupiscence is extinct.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A true spiritual person is defined as someone who abandons all worldly desires, lives without a fixed home, and has completely eliminated sensual longing.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, leaving all longings, travels about without a home, and in whom all covetousness is extinct.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A true spiritual person is defined as someone who has abandoned all desires, lives without a fixed home, and has completely eliminated greed.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, after leaving all bondage to men, has risen above all bondage to the gods, and is free from all and every bondage.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A true spiritual exemplar is someone who has freed themselves from all dependencies, including allegiance to other people and to divine powers, achieving complete liberation from every form of constraint.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has left what gives pleasure and what gives pain, who is cold, and free from all germs (of renewed life), the hero who has conquered all the worlds.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A person who has transcended both pleasure and pain, who remains unmoved by worldly conditions and has overcome all realms of existence, is the true spiritual exemplar.
rows.riskNotes: The ideal of being 'cold' and having 'conquered all worlds' sets an extreme spiritual standard that can make struggling readers feel like failures for still feeling pain or attachment, especially those already emotionally numb or grieving.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who knows the destruction and the return of beings everywhere, who is free from bondage, welfaring (Sugata), and awakened (Buddha).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A true enlightened person understands the full cycle of existence—how beings are destroyed and reborn—and lives unbound by that knowledge.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana whose path the gods do not know, nor spirits (Gandharvas), nor men, whose passions are extinct, and who is an Arhat (venerable).
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A true spiritual master is someone whose inner life is so completely transformed that supernatural beings and ordinary people alike cannot trace or comprehend their ways, because they have fully extinguished all passions and attained the highest state of liberation.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who calls nothing his own, whether it be before, behind, or between, who is poor, and free from the love of the world.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A true spiritual person owns nothing in any direction or time, lives without possessions, and has no attachment to worldly things.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana, the manly, the noble, the hero, the great sage, the conqueror, the impassible, the accomplished, the awakened.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: This passage lists ideal qualities that define an accomplished spiritual person: courage, nobility, heroic effort, wisdom, conquest over inner obstacles, unshakability, completion, and awakening.

rows.text: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who knows his former abodes, who sees heaven and hell, has reached the end of births, is perfect in knowledge, a sage, and whose perfections are all perfect.
rows.licenseNote: F. Max Müller (d. 1900), The Dhammapada, Sacred Books of the East Vol. X Part I, Oxford 1881. Public domain worldwide. Digital text transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #2017; no Project Gutenberg header or trademark text is redistributed.
rows.summary: A true spiritual master is defined as someone who remembers past lives, perceives other realms, has ended the cycle of rebirth, and attained complete knowledge and perfection.
